questions.stem: A 27-year-old man comes to student health services because of a 6-week history of increasingly severe insomnia and persistent fatigue. During this time, he also has had feelings of guilt, emptiness, hopelessness, and profound sadness. He is a graduate student and has withdrawn from most social activity. He says that during the past 4 years he has heard "Sauron" from a fantasy novel instructing him to renounce his family and forswear all sexual activity. He notes that he has had two episodes of depressed mood during the past 2 years. He began sertraline therapy during the second episode and had improvement in his mood; he discontinued the therapy 6 months ago because he was feeling well apart from intermittent hallucinations. He has no other history of serious illness and cu ently takes no medications. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has a depressed mood and restricted affect. His thought processes are mildly tangential. He does not appear to be responding to internal stimuli. He has not had suicidal ideation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features

questions.educationalObjective: Patients with schizoaffective disorder demonstrate psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and/or negative symptoms) along with significant mood episodes (major depressive, manic episodes). Unlike patients with major depressive disorder with psychotic features, patients with schizoaffective disorder experience psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood symptoms. , r,

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: According to the DSM-5, patients with schizophrenia spectrum disorders (eg, schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder) demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). Many patients also illustrate prodromal periods of strange behavior and social withdrawal. To meet diagnostic criteria, patients must demonstrate functional deficits, and the total duration of symptoms must exceed 6 months. Patients with schizoaffective disorder must demonstrate mood episodes (eg, major depressive episodes as in this patient, manic episodes) for a substantial portion of the duration of the illness. Major depressive episodes include depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or feelings of worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). However, patients with schizoaffective disorder must also experience psychotic symptoms (eg, delusions, hallucinations) in the absence of mood symptoms for at least 2 weeks. Management requires antipsychotic medication and may also include adjunctive antidepressant medication (if depressive episodes are predominant) or mood stabilizer medication (if manic episodes are predominant).

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. Dysthymic disorder (Choice A), more commonly known as persistent depressive disorde efers to depressive symptoms lasting longer than 2 years. Patients with dysthymic disorder do not meet major depressive disorder criteria (as in this patient) and do not typically demonstrate psychotic symptoms. \ j - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - • - - - - - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- - - -- - - - - - - - - - - , r, .... Patients with schizophrenia (Choice D) demonstrate similar psychotic symptoms to patients with schizoaffective disorder (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and/or negative symptoms, known as Criterion A of schizophrenia spectrum disorder diagnoses) but do not experience mood episodes. Schizotypal personality disorder (Choice E) is one of the cluster A personality disorders, the odd or eccentric cluster. The disorder is characterized by odd or magical behavior and thinking and a constricted affect. Complex, rigid delusions as in this patient would be atypical for patients with schizotypal personality disorder. Additionally, this patient's major depressive episodes are more consistent with schizoaffective disorder.

questions.retrievalTag: Fragile site on the X chromosome
questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the physician for evaluation of difficulty reading. His teache eports that he cannot keep up with his classmates. His mothe eports that he is generally well behaved but frequently flaps his hands when he is nervous. Physical examination shows a high forehead, prominent supraorbital ridges, protruding ears, and a prominent jaw. Psychoeducational testing of the patient shows an IQ of 65. Academic achievement testing is consistent with his IQ. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Decreased numbers of trinucleotide repeats of the X chromosome

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Fragile site on the X chromosome

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Increased expression of the homeobox A 1 (HOXA 1) gene

answerChoices.label: F
answerChoices.text: Increased serum concentrations of phenylpyruvic acid

questions.educationalObjective: Fragile X syndrome is an X-linked dominant cause of inherited intellectual disability that presents as a result of a CGG-trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene. It presents with clinical features including intellectual disability, an elongated face with a prominent jaw, a high-arched palate, large ears, hyperextensible joints, and postpubertal macroorchidism. ....

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient's constellation of findings, including intellectual disability, an elongated face, and large ears, is suggestive of fragile X syndrome. Patients may also present with macroorchidism, hyperextensible joints, and a high-arched palate. Fragile X syndrome is the second most common cause of inherited intellectual disability after Down syndrome and is inherited in an X-linked dominant fashion. It is caused by a CGG-trinucleotide repeat expansion within the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome. On karyotype, the end of the X chromosome appears truncated, leading to the name fragile X syndrome. Patients with fragile X syndrome are at increased risk for mitral valve prolapse and educational difficulties. They are often diagnosed with autism. As with all trinucleotide repeat expansions, genetic anticipation is seen, wherein future generations have increased severity and/or earlier onset of disease.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, C, E, F, G, H, and I. Congenital rubella (Choice A) presents in newborns with the classic triad of cataracts, sensorineural deafness, and patent ductus arteriosus. It is unlikely to be a cause of intellectual disability presenting in childhood. Increased, not decreased (Choice B), numbers of trinucleotide repeats of the X chromosome are seen in fragile X syndrome. Decreased serum hypoxanthine phosphoribosyltransferase activity (Choice C) is seen in Lesch-Nyhan syndrome. This condition is - -· . . . -· ..... ·- -. - ·- -.. . .. . .. -. -· .. .. . ·- . - - . . .. - - .... This presents with failure to thrive, recu ent seizures, metabolic acidosis, and developmental delay. It is typically identified in infancy. Instead, features of Klinefelter syndrome include dysgenesis of the seminiferous tubules and testicular atrophy, leading to decreased spermatogenesis. questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of flu-like symptoms and cough productive of clear sputum for 24 hours. He has been hospitalized eight times over the past 5 years because of biza e delusions and hallucinations during which a voice tells him what to do. He has a 9-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus. In addition to insulin, he is receiving clozapine therapy (400 mg daily) for his psychotic condition. During mental status examination, he is hallucinating and says that h,e is being pursued by special agents of the government. Leukocyte count is 2750/mm 3, and serum glucose concentration is 140 mg/dl. Urinalysis is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Clozapine is an atypical antipsychotic medication effective in treatment-refractory schizophrenia. Though clozapine ca ies a lowe isk than many antipsychotics for causing extrapyramidal symptoms, clozapine may cause serious adverse effects such as agranulocytosis, myocarditis, and seizure. In patients with suspected clozapine-induced agranulocytosis, clozapine should be discontinued. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient's leukopenia may be related to agranulocytosis, a rare but life-threatening adverse effect of clozapine therapy that leads to increased susceptibility to infection. The pathogenesis may be related to the production of reactive oxygen species in the bone ma ow. Patients on clozapine therapy must have their absolute neutrophil count monitored regularly. In suspected clozapine-induced agranulocytosis, clozapine should be discontinued. Clozapine is an atypical antipsychotic medication effective in treatment-refractory schizophrenia. Though clozapine ca ies a lowe isk than many antipsychotics of causing extrapyramidal symptoms, clozapine is associated with serious adverse effects such as agranulocytosis, myocarditis, and seizure. Clozapine may also lead to severe sedation and constipation (as a result of its anticholinergic activity) along with sialo hea (potentially also related to modulation of acetylcholine signaling).

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Adding lithium carbonate therapy (Choice A) would be ineffective in acute agranulocytosis. In patients who experience agranulocytosis but require clozapine for psychiatric stability, physicians may choose to rechallenge patients with clozapine. During a clozapine rechallenge specifically, adjunctive lithium may mitigate clozapine-related myelosuppression. Adding penicillin therapy (Choice B) would be premature in this patient without a diagnosed bacterial infection. His symptoms may be attributable to a viral uppe espiratory infection. Decreasing the dose of clozapine (Choice C) would be insufficient to allow the recovery of leukocyte counts. Clozapine should be discontinued. .... Decreasing the dose of clozapine (Choice C) would be insufficient to allow the recovery of leukocyte counts. Clozapine should be discontinued. Increasing the dose of clozapine (Choice E) is appropriate in patients with decompensated schizophrenia who do not demonstrate leukopenia. This patient demonstrates leukopenia.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his parents because "he does whatever he wants and comes and goes as he pleases." They report that he always has been challenging to manage and has not responded to disciplinary measures since he was 10 years of age. During the past 2 years, his behavior has become worse; he has been a ested twice in the past month, once for assault and once for vandalism. He is cu ently on probation. His parents state that they have found him intoxicated in his room on several occasions, and they recently found marijuana paraphernalia in his room. Today, the patient is sullen and minimally cooperative. He says he is frequently i itable and short tempered. His clothes smell of cigarette smoke. Physical examination shows several tattoos over the chest and upper extremities and several body piercings; no other abnormalities are noted. Mental status examination shows an i itable mood and poor eye contact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Conduct disorde efers to persistent, repetitive, and pervasive patterns of violating major societal norms, rules, and basic rights of others.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Conduct disorde efers to persistent emotional and behavioral problems such as purposeful aggression toward people and animals in children and adolescents. The behavioral problems can also include theft, lying, destruction of property, and serious rule violations. The diagnosis of conduct disorder can be given in children with repetitive and pervasive patterns of violating major societal norms, rules, and basic rights of others. Children with conduct disorder are at increased risk for developing antisocial personality disorder as an adult. Treatment for both conduct disorder and antisocial personality disorder includes psychotherapy such as cognitive behavioral therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, D, E, and F. Antisocial personality disorder (Choice A) is a cluster B personality disorder that arises from a deficit in empathy, resulting in pervasive violations of others' rights, aggression, and a hostile and manipulative attitude toward others. These patients may also be chronically bored and hence sensation seeking, leading to a high rate of substance abuse and gambling. It is generally diagnosed in adults, whereas the prefe ed diagnosis in children and adolescents is conduct disorder. Bipolar disorder (Choice B) is characterized by discrete episodes of depression and mania, which can sometimes be accompanied by psychotic symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions. -. . . - • • • • • . . -. - Mark F) Oppositional defiant disorder Conduct disorde efers to persistent emotional and behavioral problems such as purposeful aggression toward people and animals in children and adolescents. These patients may also be chronically bored and hence sensation seeking, leading to a high rate of substance abuse and gambling. It is generally diagnosed in adults, whereas the prefe ed diagnosis in children and adolescents is conduct disorder. Bipolar disorder (Choice B) is characterized by discrete episodes of depression and mania, which can sometimes be accompanied by psychotic symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions. Dysthymic disorder (Choice D) is characterized by persistent depressive mood and chronic depression. Symptoms of depressive and dysthymic disorders typically include depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance), which this patient does not demonstrate. Intermittent explosive disorder (Choice E) is characterized by impulsive and explosive outbursts of anger that are often disproportionate to the magnitude of provocation. Aggressive outbursts can include verbal aggression, injury, or destruction and, in contrast to conduct disorder, are not premeditated. Oppositional defiant disorder (Choice F) manifests as a persistent pattern of angry mood, defiant behavior, and vindictiveness that causes distress and impaired functioning in a child or their caretakers. This child's aggression is born out of age-appropriate impulsivity rather than defiance.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of progressive headaches that have caused him to miss several days of school. They now occur daily and are always present on awakening in the morning. He describes the headaches as a diffuse, constant pressure that occasionally throbs. He feels better after taking acetaminophen and resting in front of the television for 10 to 20 minutes. He has not noticed any particular events that cause the headaches, and he says that they are not exacerbated by light, sound, coughing, or straining. He has not had nausea or vomiting. Two months ago, he sustained a whiplash injury in a motor vehicle collision. His parents say that he snores loudly. His mother and maternal aunt have migraines, and his father has major depressive disorder. The patient is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 86 kg (189 lb); BMI is 31 kg/m2. He breathes through his mouth. Respirations are 20/min. Examination shows 3 to 5 beats of jerk nystagmus with far lateral gaze bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's headaches? -
questions.educationalObjective: Isolated headaches that occur in the morning, resolve minutes to hours after awakening, and have a pressure or squeezing type quality are frequently reported by patients with sleep apnea. Additional evaluation fo isk factors and a formal sleep study are recommended.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Headaches that occur in the morning, resolve minutes to hours after awakening, have a pressure or squeezing type quality, and an absence of other symptoms are frequently reported by patients with untreated obstructive sleep apnea (OSA). This patient is likely experiencing sleep-related hypoventilation caused by OSA. Clinical features of untreated OSA include excessive daytime fatigue, falling asleep at inappropriate times, loud snoring, and a disrupted sleep-wake cycle. Risk factors include male sex, obesity, congenital or traumatic anatomic abnormalities of the oropharynx and upper airway, neuromuscular disorders, and use of sedating medications. Untreated OSA is associated with hypertension. Management consists of lifestyle changes (weight loss, dietary modifications, increased activity, changing sleep position), avoiding sedating medications and substances, and continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) while sleeping. Second-line therapies include oral appliances such as mandibular advancement devices for symptoms refractory to CPAP use, and surgery for cases with obstructing lesions of the airway.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, C, D, and E. Hydrocephalus (Choice A) refers to abnormal enlargement of the ventricles and subarachnoid space caused by cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) accumulation, which may be a result of increased CSF production, decreased CSF absorption, or obstruction of CSF flow. Patients present with findings caused by increased intracranial pressure, which include headache, nausea, papilledema on retinal examination, ataxia, and encephalopathy. Severely increased pressure results in the Cushing triad of bradycardia, widened pulse ressure and h ertension, and i e ular breathin . Severely increased pressure results in the Cushing triad of bradycardia, widened pulse pressure and hypertension, and i egular breathing. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (Choice B), also called pseudotumor cerebri, is a diagnosis of exclusion made when patients present with symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (as described for Choice A) without an identifiable cause. Patients are typically of the female sex and of childbearing age. Risk factors include obesity and use of an oral contraceptive or vitamin A. Major depressive disorder (Choice C) may present with nonspecific symptoms such as headache, and positive family history is a risk factor. However, diagnosis requires the presence of depressed mood or anhedonia and at least four other associated symptoms (insomnia or hypersomnia, guilt, decreased energy, concentration deficits, weight loss or weight gain, psychomoto etardation, suicidal ideation). Migraine (Choice D) headaches are typically severe, unilateral, throbbing, and associated with nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. Some patients additionally experience an aura before or during the headache, which may present with vision loss followed by the perception of scintillating shapes or lines and/or paresthesias. Migraine headaches are often associated with specific triggers. School-related anxiety (Choice E) may present as nonspecific symptoms such as headache or abdominal pain in younger patients, or symptoms may be reported by the patient as an attempt to avoid the distressing event. This patient has multiple risk factors and symptoms more characteristic of sleep apnea.

questions.retrievalTag: Obtain a rectal temperature under supervision
questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman reports a 3-week history of recu ent fevers. She has a 4-year history of major depressive disorder. Her temperature is 42°C (107.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical examination is otherwise normal. Her leukocyte count is 7500/mm 3 with a normal differential. Liver function tests and serum creatine kinase activity are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in patient care?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Obtain a rectal temperature under supervision

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Order a bone ma ow biopsy

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Order a CT scan of the abdomen

questions.educationalObjective: In factitious disorder, patients consciously produce symptoms (eg, purposely injure themselves, fake a fever by eating something warm) for primary gain. A history of multiple procedures and subjective or objective findings that are inconsistent with a known disease suggests factitious disorder. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: In factitious disorder, patients consciously produce symptoms (eg, purposely injure themselves, fake a fever by eating something warm) for primary gain. Primary gain is the motivation to be cared for, which constitutes an unconscious motivator for the patient's conscious production of symptoms. Early losses, neglect, or abuse may disrupt patients' identity and relationships, and adopting the sick role may represent patients' attempts to cope with stress and stabilize their identity and relationships. Psychiatric comorbidities (eg, depression, substance use disorders, personality disorders) are common but not present in all patients. Diagnosis is supported by inconsistencies in the history or physical examination and a history of extensive clinical evaluations. This patient's isolated, severe hyperthermia without other associated vital sign changes (eg, tachycardia, hyper- or hypotension, and/or tachypnea) or physical examination or laboratory evidence of severe hyperthermia (eg, acute distress, diaphoresis, flushed skin, sequela of rhabdomyolysis such as increased creatinine or creatine kinase) is likely attributable to factitious disorder. To exclude a true fever and assist in establishing the factitious disorder diagnosis, a rectal temperature may be obtained in this patient. Treatment includes regular follow-up with one physician who oversees management (as opposed to symptom-triggered appointments) and possible psychotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. Administering broad-spectrum antibiotics (Choice A) or ordering a CT scan of the abdomen (Choice E) are likely unnecessary in this patient without focal infectious symptoms or vital signs indicative of sepsis physiology (eg, tachycardia and hypotension). The patient's temperature should be confirmed before evaluating or treating for infectious causes. Administering corticosteroids (Choice B) may treat recu ent fevers related to rare autoimmune or autoinflammatory conditions. However, it has not yet been established that the patient has a true fever, and this patient does not demonstrate other evidence of autoimmune disease on history or physical examination. - - - - - - - - - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- - - - - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - .... The patient's temperature should be confirmed before evaluating or treating for infectious causes. Administering corticosteroids (Choice B) may treat recu ent fevers related to rare autoimmune or autoinflammatory conditions. However, it has not yet been established that the patient has a true fever, and this patient does not demonstrate other evidence of autoimmune disease on history or physical examination. Ordering a bone ma ow biopsy (Choice D) would assist in evaluating for hematologic malignancy or infection as the cause of recu ent fevers. However, it has not yet been established that the patient has a true fever.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of fatigue, insomnia, and anxiety. His divorce was finalized 1 month ago, ending his 10-year ma iage to his wife, who he learned was having an affair with his best friend. He feels anger toward his ex-wife and his friend, but he says, "I know what they did was wrong and that I am a good person who got hurt by them." He has no regrets about the divorce because he believes that he never would have been able to trust her again. He is relieved that they have no children. He often thinks about whether he will rema y, which causes anxiety and prevents him from sleeping. He has had no change in appetite. He works as a middle school teacher and has missed several days of school because of tiredness. His mind wanders when he watches television o eads at home. He has decreased energy but still enjoys activities with family and friends. He has no history of medical or psychiatric illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is cooperative and engaging and exhibits a full range of affect. He says that his mood is okay. His thoughts are organized. There is no evidence of suicidal or homicidal ideation. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Adjustment disorde efers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. Treatment commonly includes brief psychotherapy, though many physicians also treat anxiety or depressive symptoms empirically. A short course of a nonbenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic medication (eg, zolpidem) may help a subset of patients with insomnia secondary to depressive or anxiety symptoms. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Adjustment disorde efers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. The emotional disturbance manifests as marked distress out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in daily functioning. Adjustment disorders are typically associated with anxiety, depression, or behavioral symptoms without meeting the criteria for anxiety or depressive disorders. For example, this patient demonstrates some neurovegetative symptoms (eg, decreased sleep and energy) and trouble concentrating but not depressed mood or anhedonia. As adjustment disorder is typically self-limited, the treatment of adjustment disorder commonly includes brief supportive psychotherapy, though brief pharmacotherapy that targets associated anxiety or depressive symptoms may also be empirically provided. In young patients with severe insomnia and without substance use disorders, nonbenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic medications (eg, zolpidem, zaleplon, eszopiclone) may be appropriate. Given that nonbenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic medications increase the risk for falls, sedation, cognitive impairment, and addiction, these medications should be used with caution in older patients and patients with substance use disorders.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers

questions.stem: One month after undergoing liver transplantation, a 4 7-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital for treatment of organ rejection. Twenty-four hours after intravenous methylprednisolone therapy is begun, she begins to have visual hallucinations that her organ donor has entered her hospital room naked and is drawing symbols on her skin with an eyebrow pencil. Cu ent medications include tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, omeprazole, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, acyclovir, and nystatin. The patient has no history of psychiatric illness. She is awake and alert. Physical examination shows a well-healing surgical incision over the abdomen. On mental status examination, she says she is wo ied about her visual hallucinations. She is oriented to person, place, and time. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's psychiatric symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Psychosis presents with delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thought and speech, and agitation or hyperarousal. It can be caused by a primary psychiatric disorder, primary medical condition, substance use, or medication use. Corticosteroids are a common cause of medication-induced psychosis. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Systemic corticosteroids have many adverse effects, including neuropsychiatric effects such as psychosis and mood disorders. These adverse effects are usually reversible with reduction of dosing, although some require treatment. Psychosis describes an episode in which a patient may experience delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thought and speech, and agitation or hyperarousal. It may be caused by a primary psychiatric disorder (eg, schizophrenia), a primary medical condition, or substance use, including medications. Common causes of substance-induced psychosis include alcohol, anticholinergics, antiparkinsonian medications, and corticosteroids. This patient without a psychiatric history is experiencing visual hallucinations in the context of recent corticosteroid exposure, making corticosteroid-induced psychotic disorder the most likely explanation for her symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine examination. He is accompanied by his wife. During the examination, his wife states that he is competitive, rigid, stubborn, and opinionated. She feels he has difficulty making simple decisions and discarding old items that are of no use and is anxious and obsessed with being early for everything. He is a successful lawyer, and his coworkers have said that he believes that his way is the only way of getting things done. When asked about this, he does not think there is a problem. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Obsessive-compulsive personality disorder is a cluster C personality disorder, the anxious or fearful cluster. It is characterized by excessively rigid routines, perfectionism, stubbornness, and an obsession with control that interferes with the patient's social function. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive personality disorder is a cluster C personality disorder, the anxious or fearful cluster. It is characterized by excessively rigid routines and perfectionism. Patients often display an obsession with control and the belief that their approach to a specific problem or situation is the only co ect approach. A patient's family, friends, or coworkers often note rigid, stubborn thought patterns. This rigidity and obsession with perfection may occur at the expense of a patient's social relationships or career, as evidenced by this patient's difficulty making simple decisions. Patients with obsessive-compulsive personality disorder do not display intrusive, recu ent thoughts or compulsive behaviors as seen in obsessive-compulsive disorder, which is an anxiety disorder, not a personality disorder. Personality disorders are persistently maladaptive ways of relating to the self and to society that typically appear by early adulthood.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice A) features excessive and persistent wo ying that may be associated with sympathetic hyperactivity. Patients do not typically display excessive orderliness, rigidity, or perfectionism. Narcissistic personality disorder (Choice B) is a cluster B personality disorder, the emotional or dramatic cluster, that is characterized by fragile self-esteem and compensatory a ogant, self-aggrandizing behavior to gain admiration, sometimes at others' expense. Panic disorder (Choice D) features acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and is associated with symptoms of sympathetic hyperactivity such as tachycardia, heart palpitations, sweating, and trembling along with shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, gastrointestinal distress, and paresthesias. Panic disorder is characterized by recu ent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with wo y about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. .... questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department from a psychiatric hospital because of a decreasing level of consciousness over the past 3 days. He has become progressively less responsive and has remained in bed for the past 12 hours without speaking or following commands. He has a history of schizophrenia with multiple psychiatric hospitalizations and is cu ently being treated with haloperidol and benztropine. His temperature is 39.4 °C (103°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 180/100 mm Hg. He withdraws weakly to noxious stimuli. His eyes are open, but he does not fix or follow visual stimuli or gaze around the room. He is mute and does not follow commands. Examination shows marked rigidity of the neck and all extremities. Laboratory studies show: Leukocyte count 18,000/mm 3 Segmented neutrophils 80% Lymphocytes 20% Serum creatine kinase 12,000 U/L Cerebrospinal fluid Erythrocyte count Leukocyte count Protein Glucose 2/mm 3 1/mm 3 28 mg/dL 78 mg/dL A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause?
questions.educationalObjective: NMS is a life-threatening condition associated with the antagonism of dopamine that presents with altered mental status, muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, and autonomic dysfunction. Characteristic laboratory studies show leukocytosis and Mark • • • • • g g p p ' g y, hyperthermia, and autonomic dysfunction. Common causative agents are antipsychotics (especially high-potency antipsychotics such as haloperidol) and antidopaminergic nausea medications. Rigidity results from dopamine antagonism in the nigrostriatal pathway, while autonomic dysfunction and hyperthermia may result from dopamine antagonism in the hypothalamus. Characteristic laboratory studies show leukocytosis and increased serum creatine kinase, lactate dehydrogenase, and alkaline phosphatase (caused by prolonged muscle rigidity and consequent myolysis). Treatment is supportive and includes discontinuation of the offending medication, hydration, cooling blankets, and benzodiazepines if needed for agitation. Dantrolene is potentially beneficial. Inco ect Answers: A, B, C, E, F, and G. Catatonia (Choice A) is a neurobehavioral syndrome marked by the inability to move or speak or the inability to stop moving or speaking repetitively that typically arises from an exacerbation of a psychiatric disorder. In a severe form of catatonia called malignant catatonia, patients additionally demonstrate autonomic instability, which can present similarly to NMS. However, this patient's lack of preceding psychiatric symptoms or stereotyped movements make malignant catatonia less likely. Conversion reaction (Choice B), also refe ed to as functional neurologic symptom disorder, can result in loss of motor or sensory function that cannot be explained by a physical illness or injury. Patients are often aware of the symptoms but are indifferent about their significance. Dissociative fugue (Choice C) is a subtype of dissociative amnesia in which a patient cannot recall important autobiographical information and is associated with sudden unexpected travel or wandering in a dissociated state. The amnesia is often related to stressful or traumatic events. Infection (Choice E) such as meningitis typically presents with headache, fever, photophobia, phonophobia, and neck stiffness, with possible Kernig or Brudzinski signs on examination. It may be caused by bacterial, viral, fungal or parasitic infections. This patient's cerebrospinal fluid leukocyte count is within the reference range at <5/mm 3 and is reassuring against bacterial meningitis. Malingering (Choice F) is defined by falsification of symptoms to obtain a secondary gain. This patient's symptoms have another possible explanation, NMS, and there is no clear secondary gain that he might obtain. Several toxins (Choice G) in overdose can result in hyperthermia. For example, sympathomimetics such as cocaine and methamphetamine can lead to hyperthermia in several pathways including brown adipose tissue activation, skeletal muscle contractions, and dopamine antagonism. NMS is a more likely explanation in this patient with muscle rigidity and fever in the setting of taking haloperidol. questions.stem: A 57-year-old woman comes to the physician because of difficulty sleeping, tearfulness, and restlessness since her daughter was diagnosed with metastatic breast cancer 3 days ago. She reports that when she goes to bed at night, she is unable to fall asleep for several hours and lays in bed wo ying about her daughter's situation. The patient underwent a mastectomy for breast cancer 7 years ago. She takes acetaminophen/butalbital for occasional migraines. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is tearful and tense but calms during the conversation. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Adjustment disorde efers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. Treatment commonly includes brief psychotherapy, though many physicians also treat anxiety or depressive symptoms empirically. A short course of benzodiazepines may help a subset of patients with severe anxiety symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Adjustment disorde efers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. The emotional disturbance manifests as marked distress out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in daily functioning. Adjustment disorders are typically associated with anxiety, depression, or behavioral symptoms without meeting the criteria of anxiety or depressive disorders. As adjustment disorder is typically self-limited, the treatment commonly includes brief supportive psychotherapy, though brief pharmacotherapy that targets associated symptoms may be empirically provided. In patients with severe anxiety symptoms, benzodiazepines may be appropriate. Physicians should prescribe benzodiazepines judiciously because of the potential for addiction. Compared with other benzodiazepines, clonazepam is less likely to lead to addiction because of its slow onset and decreased degree of euphoria.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, D, E, F, G, and H. Biofeedback has been utilized for headaches, anxiety disorders (eg, post-traumatic stress disorder), attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, and other conditions, but not adjustment disorder specifically. Carbamazepine therapy (Choice B), a mood stabilizer that decreases sodium influx into neurons, is utilized for bipolar disorder and convulsions. This patient's difficulty sleeping is related to anxiety rather than mania or depression. Clonidine therapy (Choice D), an a 2-adrenergic agonist, is used for hypertension, for opioid withdrawal, and off-label for anxiety. Clonidine has not been studied in patients with adjustment disorder. Imipramine therapy (Choice E), a tricyclic antidepressant, and sertraline therapy (Choice H), a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor, are antidepressants that may be used to empirically address depressive symptoms of adjustment disorder. However, this patient has predominant anxiety symptoms. Olanzapine therapy (Choice F), an antipsychotic medication with mood stabilizing properties, is typically used for bipolar disorder and schizophrenia. It causes metabolic adverse effects and is not a first-line agent for anxiety. Pentobarbital therapy (Choice G), a barbiturate, is used as an anticonvulsant. It may result in anxiolysis but can cause dangerous respiratory depression and typically requires blood pressure and cardiac monitoring. Barbiturates are not regularly used as psychiatric treatments.

questions.stem: A previously healthy 23-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by friends 20 minutes after the onset of biza e behavior that began at a party. His friends say that he became paranoid and started threatening them. His temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F). Physical examination shows agitation, diaphoresis, horizontal nystagmus, and occasional myoclonus. Mental status examination shows dysarthric speech and combativeness. His thoughts are disorganized, and he is unable to give a coherent history. He believes that the physician is trying to kill him. Thirty minutes later, he becomes somnolent. Administration of naloxone does not improve his condition. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Phencyclidine, also known as PCP, is an NMDA glutamatergic receptor antagonist that is used recreationally for its euphoria-inducing and dissociative effects. PCP intoxication is often characterized by signs and symptoms including hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia, e atic behavior, paranoia, psychosis, aggression, decreased sensation, ataxia, nystagmus, myoclonus, and dysarthria. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Phencyclidine, also known as PCP, is an NMDA glutamatergic receptor antagonist that is used recreationally for its euphoria-inducing and dissociative effects. PCP intoxication is often characterized by signs and symptoms including hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia, e atic behavior, paranoia, psychosis, aggression, decreased sensation, ataxia, nystagmus, myoclonus, and dysarthria. Patients may require sedation, treatment with antipsychotic medications, or physical restraint until their symptoms subside. Withdrawal symptoms following PCP intoxication are uncommon. Naloxone has no effect on the pathways involved in PCP intoxication.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Alcohol intoxication (Choice A) presents with sedation, altered mentation, dysarthria, and vomiting. Alcohol withdrawal may lead to severe complications including delirium tremens and seizures. Alcohol intoxication does not typically present with nystagmus; hyperthermia and autonomic instability would also be uncommon in acute intoxication, though the vasodepressive and sedating effects can result in hypotension and bradypnea. Amphetamine intoxication (Choice B) may resemble PCP intoxication and also presents with hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia, e atic behavior, and paranoid or delusional thinking. However, it does not typically present with nystagmus, ataxia, or myoclonus. Opioid intoxication (Choice C) may result in e atic behavior, miosis, respiratory depression, and obtundation but should respond rapidly to administration of naloxone. questions.stem: A 37-year-old man comes to the physician because of increasing distress about his procrastination and indecision. He has been late for work often because he is easily distracted by any task; as a result, he has received poor job performance ratings. He a ived late for today's examination because he was awake late last night creating a new computer folder system and then had to refold the clothes in his closet several times this morning. He says he becomes nervous if he is unable to finish similar minor projects. He sometimes daydreams about projects and must complete them in his daydreams before attending to other tasks. He reports that his family is exasperated by his behavior. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He appears fatigued. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is fully oriented. He is cooperative but fidgets and has poor eye contact. He is able to repeat six digits forward, spell "world" backward, and perform serial sevens. He gives four interpretations of a proverb and is unable to decide on a final answer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: OCD manifests with recu ent, intrusive urges or thoughts (obsessions) that the patient tries to relieve or neutralize by repetitive rituals (compulsions). Common symptom complexes revolve around cleanliness, symmetry, harm to self or others, taboo thoughts or ideas, and completeness. Symptoms are time-consuming, cause significant distress, and markedly impair functioning. Treatment is with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, clomipramine, or venlafaxine, in addition to cognitive behavioral therapy. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) manifests as recu ent, intrusive urges or thoughts (obsessions) that the patient tries to relieve or neutralize by repetitive rituals (compulsions). These symptoms are time-consuming and cause significant anxiety to the patient and markedly impair functioning. Compulsions can occur in response to a fear of "incompleteness," which requires repetitive actions until a task is considered complete, as in this patient. Other common obsessive-compulsion symptoms are related to cleaning, symmetry, harm to self or others, and taboo thoughts or ideas. For example, a classic case of OCD is a patient with obsessions regarding cleanliness and contamination, whose compulsions include excessive bathing or hand washing. Patients with OCD also commonly experience anxiety or major mood disorders. It typically presents relatively early in life, with most cases present by age 20 years. It is commonly treated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, clomipramine, or venlafaxine, in addition to cognitive behavioral therapy.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after he was found wandering on the street. The officers say that the patient was screaming and talking to himself. He is unkempt and agitated, and he is wearing winter clothes on a hot summer day. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. During the examination, he says that he is not going to take any medicine, and the "television told me about the murders." Which of the following is the most likely additional finding in this patient?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Cerebral edema on CT scan of the head

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Enlarged lateral and third ventricles on CT scan of the head

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Increased serum and urine copper concentrations

questions.educationalObjective: Patients with schizophrenia demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). As a result of excessive synaptic pruning during development, patients may demonstrate enlarged lateral and third ventricles secondary to cortical atrophy on . . neuro1maging. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: According to the DSM-5, patients with schizophrenia demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). Many patients also illustrate prodromal periods of strange behavior and social withdrawal. To meet diagnostic criteria, patients must demonstrate functional deficits, and the total duration of symptoms must exceed 6 months. In recent decades, schizophrenia has been postulated to result from excessive pruning of glutamatergic synapses in the cerebral cortex during critical periods of development, explaining the negative and cognitive symptoms of schizophrenia (glutamatergic hypofunction may then dysregulate mesolimbic dopamine to produce the positive symptoms of schizophrenia). As a result of synapse loss and neuronal atrophy in the cortex, enlarged lateral and third ventricles may be demonstrated on neuroimaging. Treatment requires antipsychotic medications.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, C, D, and E. Cerebral edema on CT scan of the head (Choice A) arises from a variety of causes including trauma, ischemic stroke, intracranial masses, and hydrocephalus. Cerebral edema does not typically occur in schizophrenia. Increased serum dopamine concentration (Choice C) is unlikely, since dopamine increases in schizophrenia are specific to certain regions of the brain only (eg, mesolimbic pathway). Dopamine in the central nervous system (CNS) does not cross the blood-brain ba ier to the systemic vasculature. Increased serum and urine copper concentrations (Choice D) are typical of Wilson disease. Wilson disease is a rare condition of copper accumulation throughout the body (including the CNS) and may present with psychosis. This patient lacks other signs of Wilson disease such as liver disease or ocular findings, and primary psychotic disorders such as schizophrenia are more common. - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - .... questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the physician because of a 5-month history of increasingly severe episodes of lower abdominal pain. The pain is exacerbated during bowel movements, sexual intercourse, and menses. Treatment with several analgesics has not controlled the pain. Laparoscopy 4 years ago and a second laparoscopy 2 years ago for evaluation of pain showed no abnormalities. She is sexually active with one male partner, and they use condoms for contraception. She is a single parent and lost her job 6 months ago. She now has difficulty sleeping 1 to 3 nights each week because she wo ies about finding a job. Abdominal examination shows tenderness in both lower quadrants. Pelvic examination shows a mobile, nontender uterus and normal, nontender adnexa. A complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, Pap smear, and urine and cervical cultures show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to determine the cause of this patient's pain?
questions.educationalObjective: In somatic symptom disorder, the patient is excessively preoccupied with one or more somatic symptoms such that these symptoms disrupt daily life. These symptoms may or may not originate from an underlying disease, though they are typically unconsciously produced or exacerbated by psychological factors such as stress. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Somatic symptom disorder, formerly refe ed to as somatoform disorder, is the most likely cause of this patient's pain with ongoing symptoms despite an extensive evaluation and may be diagnosed on psychiatric assessment. According to the DSM-5, patients with somatic symptom disorder are preoccupied with an existing somatic symptom such that these symptoms disrupt the patient's daily life. These symptoms may or may not originate from an underlying illness, though they are typically unconsciously produced or exacerbated by psychological factors such as stress, as in this patient. Patients with somatic symptom disorder persistently devote excessive time and energy to these symptoms o elated health concerns (eg, repeatedly going to the doctor). Somatic symptom disorder typically demonstrates a chronic and fluctuating course. Management of somatic symptom disorder centers around regular primary care follow- up that targets coping skills, provides reassurance, and avoids unnecessary tests.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, C, D, and E. MRI of the pelvis (Choice A) is unlikely to demonstrate a cause of this patient's pain. questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of poor academic performance since her family relocated to a new town 6 months ago. The patient is halfway through the eighth grade and has been earning mostly D's and F's in school. She is having trouble making friends, especially since she lost her place on the school track team because of her poor academic performance. In her last school, she earned mostly A's and B's and had many friends. The family moved after the sudden death of the patient's father as a result of a motor vehicle collision in which the patient was a passenger. The patient had sustained minor injuries that were successfully treated. Since the family moved, she has been having frequent nightmares, is easily startled, and has trouble concentrating. Her mother says that her daughter seems to be "walking around in a cloud" much of the time. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, the patient fidgets and is somewhat slow to respond to questions. She is oriented to person, place, and time. She says she feels nervous all of the time. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: In post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), patients experience intrusive memories, hyperarousal, avoidance, and negative mood/thoughts that endure for longer than 1 month secondary to a traumatic event such as a life-threatening motor vehicle collision. Psychotherapy and/or antidepressant medication can address PTSD symptoms. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a syndrome of psychological consequences of direct or witnessed exposure to serious physical or sexual violence or trauma. Though some patients recover quickly from traumatic events, vulnerable patients may develop PTSD. Patients with PTSD experience intrusive thoughts/memories (eg, nightmares or flashbacks), dysregulation of arousal (eg, restlessness, nervousness, easy startle, decreased concentration), avoidance of reminders, and negative mood/thoughts (eg, psychomoto etardation, feelings of detachment). To meet diagnostic criteria for PTSD, these symptoms must last longer than 1 month and cause social or occupational dysfunction. Treatment typically includes psychotherapy (eg, exposure therapy) and/or antidepressant medication.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Acute stress disorder (Choice A) refers to symptoms of intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, dissociation, avoidance, and/or negative mood/thoughts that occur within 1 month of a traumatic event such as a life-threatening motor vehicle collision. This patient has been symptomatic for more than 1 month. Adjustment disorder (Choice B) refers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within 3 months of an identifiable stressor, manifesting as marked distress out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in daily functioning. By definition, patients with adjustment disorder do not meet criteria for other anxiety disorders, and this patient meets diagnostic criteria for PTSD. .... questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 2 hours after cutting her arms superficially with a razor blade. On a ival, she appears calm and is holding a stuffed animal. She says that she cut herself because she wanted to "feel something" and is unsure if she will be able to stop herself from doing this again. She says that she sleeps 8 hours each night and has a good appetite. From the ages of 10 to 15 years, she was physically and sexually abused by her stepfather and mother on several occasions. She has never had a long-term relationship and has been fired from several jobs because of difficulty getting along with her supervisors. Her pulse is 80/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 120/60 mm Hg. Physical examination shows lacerations over both forearms. Neurologic examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she denies depressed mood or suicidal ideation but is convinced that she is worthless and deserves to die. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: BPD is a cluster B personality disorder that features an unstable sense of self, emotional dysregulation, and tumultuous relationships. To unconsciously fulfill emotional needs, patients with BPD make suicidal gestures, avoid abandonment, and either idealize or devalue other people.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a cluster B personality disorder, the emotional or dramatic cluster, that features an unstable sense of self and tumultuous relationships. Likely caused by a combination of genetic polymorphisms in serotonin and dopamine receptors and emotional invalidation or abuse during childhood, patients with BPD unconsciously learn to make impulsive and dramatic gestures, including self-harm, to obtain attention and emotional fulfillment from others. As a result of inadequate emotional attunement from caregivers, patients with BPD do not learn to label, understand, o egulate their emotions. Their negative emotional experiences are so intensely painful that people with BPD must separate these experiences from their consciousness. This splitting leads people with BPD to black-and-white thinking that includes seeing people as all good or all bad. Because of affective dysfunction and hypodopaminergic tone, patients may use substances or self-harm (producing endorphins) to artificially create a positive affect. Chronic invalidation also leads to poor self-esteem (eg, worthlessness) and the consequent reliance on others for self-esteem needs, resulting in an intense fear of abandonment and severe distress when abandonment happens. The inability to self-soothe and difficulty being alone commonly leads to attachment to transitional objects (eg, stuffed animals).

questions.stem: An 87-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of dehydration and emaciation. He appears catatonic and is unresponsive to questions. One month ago, he began withdrawing from relatives and talking constantly about death. He gradually stopped eating and drinking and has had a 9-kg (20-lb) weight loss during this time. He has a 35-year history of major depressive disorder and has attempted suicide twice. He has no chronic medical conditions and cu ently takes no medications. He appears cachectic and is staring into space. He does not respond to verbal stimuli. He is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 50 kg (110 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. Examination shows temporal wasting. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management? --
questions.educationalObjective: Acute catatonia is characterized by immobility, mutism, stupor, staring, and negativism, and it is often associated with an underlying psychiatric or general medical condition. Acute treatment options include benzodiazepines and electroconvulsive therapy. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Catatonia is a syndrome of severe psychomotor disturbances, classically characterized by immobility, mutism, stupor, staring, and absent responses to questions and instructions (negativism). It is frequently associated with an underlying psychiatric or general medical disorder. Delayed diagnosis can lead to poor outcomes. This patient displays evidence of severe malnutrition related to catatonia in the setting of major depressive disorder. He should be admitted to the hospital and treated promptly with a benzodiazepine or electroconvulsive therapy. Fluid resuscitation, electrolyte repletion, and careful monitoring of laboratory studies fo efeeding syndrome should be performed as well. Both benzodiazepine and electroconvulsive therapy are considered safe and effective in the treatment of catatonia. Once the acute catatonic episode has resolved, additional treatment for the patient's underlying major depressive disorder should be initiated.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old primigravid woman at 16 weeks' gestation has had increased episodes of crying, difficulty falling asleep, decreased energy, and loss of appetite over the past 6 weeks. She has a history of recu ent major depressive disorder. Her family is concerned that she might inadvertently harm herself or her fetus. Her husband reports that she has lost interest in daily activities. She states that she has no suicidal thoughts. She is concerned about her ability to take care of a newborn and is questioning her decision not to terminate the pregnancy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Reassurance with return visit in 2 weeks

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Explanation of the risks and benefits of antidepressant therapy

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Explanation of the risks and benefits of therapeutic abortion

questions.educationalObjective: Symptoms of MDD in pregnancy include 2 or more weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal or infanticidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). In gravid patients with MDD, physicians should discuss the risks and benefits of starting an SSRI medication versus not treating the MDD. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient meets criteria for major depressive disorder (MDD). Symptoms of MDD in pregnancy include 2 or more weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal or infanticidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms disrupt everyday functioning. It is recommended that patients with recu ent major depressive episodes be treated with antidepressant medication indefinitely. In gravid patients with moderate to severe depressive symptoms, selective serotonin inhibitors (SSRIs) and psychotherapy are generally recommended, since the risks of untreated depression (eg, neglect, suicide, infanticide) typically outweigh the risk of fetal adverse effects related to antidepressant medications. SSRIs are not considered major teratogens, though third trimester use may lead to mild neonatal adjustment syndrome and, rarely, persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn. In gravid patients with MDD, physicians should discuss the risks and benefits of starting an SSRI medication versus not treating the MDD.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother because of a 20-minute episode of severe chest pain and sweating that started 1 hour ago while he was watching television. On a ival, he is alert and repeatedly expresses concern that something is "very wrong." He reports that during the episode, his heart was "pounding," he felt short of breath, and he was sure he was having a heart attack and was going to die. One month ago, he had a similar episode that occu ed in the evening approximately 30 minutes after his high school football practice. At that time, results of laboratory studies and an ECG obtained in the emer,gency department showed no abnormalities. After the initial episode, he quit the football team, and he has continued to limit his physical activity because he is afraid of bringing on another episode. He reports no insomnia or changes in appetite. His mother states that he was diagnosed with an innocent heart murmur at the age of 6 months, but it has not been noted on examinations since the age of 1 year. His paternal grandfather died of a myocardial infarction 3 years ago at the age of 68 years. The patient takes no medications. He does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. He performs well in school. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 73 kg (161 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 98/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 130/83 mm Hg. Cardiac examination shows a normal S 1 and S2 with variation of S2 on respiration. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and is associated with symptoms of sympathetic hyperactivity such as tachycardia, heart palpitations, sweating, and trembling along with shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, gastrointestinal distress, and paresthesias. Panic disorder is characterized by recu ent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with wo y about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Panic disorder features panic attacks that occur unexpectedly and are associated with changes in behavior to avoid panic attack triggers. This patient is likely experiencing recu ent panic attacks consistent with panic disorder. Panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and is associated with four additional physical symptoms or associated mental states. These additional symptoms may include heart palpitations or tachycardia, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, the sensation of choking, gastrointestinal distress, paresthesias, sweating, chills, trembling, derealization, the fear of dying, and/or the fear of losing control. Panic disorder is a relatively common psychiatric disorder characterized by recu ent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with wo y about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. Treatment typically includes psychotherapy, long-term selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and short-term benzodiazepines.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old law school graduate is caught cheating on the bar examination. Further investigation shows previous allegations of cheating in junior high school and an adolescence characterized by occasional truancy, frequent fights, and lying. As an adult, he has not been able to hold a job, has been bankrupt once, and was divorced because of failure to provide proper care for his children. He is angry about the accusation of cheating and insists that the incident is a misunderstanding. He has many friends. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Antisocial personality disorder is a cluster B personality disorder, the emotional or dramatic cluster, that arises from a deficit in empathy, resulting in pervasive violations of others' rights, aggression, and a hostile and manipulative attitude toward others. This pattern of behavior often manifests as a violation of social rules and norms, including criminality and dishonesty. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Antisocial personality disorder is a cluster B personality disorder, the emotional or dramatic cluster, that arises from a deficit in empathy, resulting in pervasive violations of others' rights, aggression, and a hostile and manipulative attitude toward others. Similar to other cluster B personality disorders, patients with antisocial personality disorder struggle to regulate their emotions in that their frustration tolerance is low and they frequently behave impulsively. These patients may also be chronically bored and hence sensation seeking, leading to a high rate of substance use disorder and gambling. These personality traits lead to deficits in personal (extreme ego- centrism) and interpersonal (empathy, intimacy) functioning. Personality disorders are persistently maladaptive ways of relating to the self and to society that typically appear by early adulthood.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: B, C, D, and E. Borderline personality disorder (Choice B) is a cluster B personality disorder, the emotional or dramatic cluster, that features an unstable sense of self and tumultuous relationships. Patients may demonstrate splitting, and often have a history of self-harm (eg, cutting behavior). Patients with borderline personality disorder may experience transient psychosis when emotionally dysregulated; delusions and hallucinations are atypical. Dissociative fugue (Choice C) is characterized by discrete episodes of amnesia, including loss of one's identity, in association with sudden travel or bewildered wandering. Dysthymic disorder (Choice D), also known as persistent depressive disorder, is characterized by signs and symptoms of depression that do not meet criteria for major depressive disorder and which occur on most days over a period of at least 2 years. questions.retrievalTag: Provide information about the range of reactions to trauma
questions.stem: A 37-year-old male police officer comes to the physician at the request of his superiors 1 week after he witnessed a te orist bombing during which several civilians and three fellow police officers were killed. He sustained only minor injuries and assisted in rescuing survivors and gathering body parts. Since the bombing, he has felt emotionally numb and has been unable to enjoy activities he used to find pleasurable. He has continued to work but has requested assignments fa emoved from the site of the attack. He describes his sleep as fitful. Two years ago, he sustained a bullet wound to the right calf, which left him with a slight limp. He has no other history of medical or psychiatric illness. He says he used to drink one to two beers nightly but has been drinking two to three beers nightly for the past week. Physical examination confirms the previous gunshot injury to the calf and shows no other abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is i itable and says he is not depressed. He tells the physician, "They made me come. I'm not interested in talking with anyone. I just want to spend time with my buddies and be left alone." Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Encourage the patient to discuss the trauma in detail

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Provide information about the range of reactions to trauma

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Recommend group therapy with other trauma survivors

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Recommend a 1-month medical leave of absence from active duty

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Recommend a physician-led trauma debriefing series

questions.educationalObjective: Acute stress disorder presents similarly to post-traumatic stress disorder, with symptoms of intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, dissociation, avoidance, and/or depressed mood within 1 month of a traumatic event. Cognitive behavioral therapy may be helpful. Education of patients regarding the range of symptoms experienced after trauma may help them identify when professional assistance is needed. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient has had a recent exposure to trauma 1 week ago and is now experiencing emotional numbness, anhedonia, avoidance, and self-medication with alcohol. These findings suggest a diagnosis of acute stress disorder. Acute stress disorder presents similarly to post-traumatic stress disorder, with symptoms of intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, dissociation, avoidance, and/or depressed mood. However, acute stress disorder presents within 1 month of a traumatic event whereas post-traumatic stress disorder lasts over a month beyond the event. It typically lasts between 3 days and 1 month. Pharmacotherapy is typically not indicated, but cognitive behavioral therapy may be helpful. In this case, providing information about the range of reactions to trauma will be most helpful as it will educate the patient and may prompt him to seek help for his symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, C, D, E, F, G, and H. Encouraging the patient to discuss the trauma in detail (Choice A) is not appropriate at this time. Trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy is an effective therapy in treating acute stress disorder but does not include discussing the trauma in detail. .... Trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy is an effective therapy in treating acute stress disorder but does not include discussing the trauma in detail. Cu ently, the patient is resistant to discussing his reaction to the recent trauma any further. Recommending group therapy with other trauma survivors (Choice C), a 1-month medical leave of absence from active duty (Choice D), or a physician-led trauma debriefing series (Choice E) will not likely be effective and may create furthe esistance from the patient to discuss his experiences with his physician. Recommending a 12-step program (Choice F) is an appropriate treatment for alcohol use disorder or substance use disorder. While further discussion may show that this patient has alcohol use disorder, the most appropriate initial step is to discuss the range of reactions to trauma. Beginning clonazepam therapy (Choice G) or fluoxetine therapy (Choice H) is not appropriate at this time. Pharmacotherapy is not typically needed for acute stress disorder. Furthermore, insisting on initiating therapy in this resistant patient will not be helpful in establishing a trusting relationship between physician and patient.

questions.stem: A previously healthy 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his girlfriend because of a 6-week history of daily episodes of hearing a voice telling him that he is Christ. He is convinced that he has the power to heal the sick and requests that he be admitted into the wards to help the patients. During the past 4 weeks, he has had a 10-kg (22-lb) weight loss. He has not slept more than 2 hours nightly for 2 weeks. When he is awake at night, he watches television or calls strangers on the telephone. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 80 kg (176 lb); BMI is 27 kg/m2. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 160/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. On mental status examination, he appears restless and agitated. He flirts with female staff members and tries to convince people of his powers. He has pressured speech and talks incessantly. There is no evidence of visual hallucinations. Recall is difficult to assess because he cannot concentrate. Long-term memory is intact. He is oriented to person, place, and time. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features

questions.educationalObjective: Bipolar I disorder consists of episodes of mania, frequently in addition to hypomanic and depressive episodes. Manic episodes are characterized by a persistently elevated mood with increased energy, increased self-esteem, decreased sleep, pressured speech, racing thoughts, distractibility, psychomotor agitation or hyperactivity, and disinhibition or impulsivity (eg, gambling, unprotected sexual activity, frivolous spending). Symptoms must be present for at least 1 week o equire hospitalization.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Bipolar disorder can be divided into two subtypes. Bipolar I disorder presents with episodes of mania and frequently includes hypomanic and depressive episodes. Bipolar II disorder, in contrast, always presents with hypomanic and depressive episodes, but no manic episodes. These episodes can transition from one to the other, or the patient can be euthymic between episodes. According to the DSM-5, mania is characterized by a persistently elevated or i itable mood with increased energy that lasts at least 1 week o equires hospitalization. It is accompanied by three or more of the following: increased self-esteem, decreased sleep, pressured speech, racing thoughts, distractibility, psychomotor agitation or hyperactivity, and disinhibition or impulsivity (eg, gambling, unprotected sexual activity, frivolous spending). Delusions and hallucinations can also occur during manic episodes. Hypomanic episodes are similar to manic episodes but are less severe and do not markedly impair social functioning o equire hospitalization. Depressive episodes can also occur with bipolar disorder and are classically characterized by depressed mood, anhedonia, excessive feelings of guilt, changes to sleep patterns (eg, sleeping too much or too little), decreased energy, poor concentration, decreased appetite, psychomotor slowing, and suicidal ideation. This patient is experiencing a manic episode with psychotic features based on auditory hallucinations, delusions of grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, psychomotor agitation, pressured speech, and flirtatious behavior, making bipolar disorder the likely diagnosis. Severe episodes require hospitalization and are treated with lithium, valproate, or an antipsychotic medication.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: B, C, D, and E. - - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- - - -- - - - - - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - ....
