Cyclothymic disorder (Choice B) presents with hypomanic and depressive symptoms, which fail to meet diagnostic criteria for a hypomanic or depressive episode. Symptoms must last for at least 2 years and must not cause significant impairment. This patient's symptoms are consistent with a manic episode, which makes bipolar disorder more likely. Major depressive disorder with psychotic features (Choice C) presents classically with depressed mood, anhedonia, feelings of guilt, insomnia or hypersomnia, fatigue, impaired concentration, anorexia, psychomotor slowing, and/or suicidal ideations, in addition to psychotic features, such as delusions or hallucinations. While this patient has hallucinations and delusions, these are in the context of a manic episode. Schizoaffective disorder (Choice D) consists of schizophrenia in addition to a mood disorder, such as major depressive disorder or bipolar disorder. However, psychotic features must also exist for at least 2 weeks in absence of mood symptoms. This patient is having hallucinations during a manic episode, which does not meet diagnostic criteria for schizoaffective disorder. Schizophrenia (Choice E) has two clusters of symptoms: positive (addition of mental dysfunction including delusions, hallucinations, and disorganized thinking and behavior) and negative (absence of functions such as affect, volition, thought, and speech). This patient is experiencing delusions and hallucinations, but these are consistent with bipolar disorder.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy has had temperatures to 38.9°C (102°F), cough, and coryza for 2 days and visual hallucinations of animals and people for 2 hours. His mother has been giving him an over-the-counter cold medicine for 24 hours, but it has not relieved his symptoms. His temperature is 38.8°C (101.8°F). Examination shows a clear nasal discharge. Which of the following is the most likely reason for the child's hallucinations?
questions.educationalObjective: Dextromethorphan can act as a dissociative hallucinogen when taken in excess. Patients may present with hallucinations, mania, panic, and agitation. Dextromethorphan can cause adverse effects including nausea, vomiting, insomnia, mydriasis, fever, tachycardia, and sedation. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Dextromethorphan is a common ingredient in antitussive medications such as cough medicine. It antagonizes N-methyl-d-aspartate glutamate receptors and, when used in excess, has mild opioid effects and can act as a dissociative hallucinogen. Patients with toxicity can present with hallucinations, mania, panic, and agitation. Dextromethorphan can also cause adverse effects including nausea, vomiting, insomnia, mydriasis, fever, tachycardia, and sedation. Dextromethorphan should not be given to patients taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors because of its potential for severe interaction with resulting autonomic instability. Treatment is supportive, with restraint and sedation for agitation, cooling measures for hyperthermia, and fluids fo habdomyolysis.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, C, D, and E. Behavioral abnormalities (Choice A) would not be an appropriate diagnosis, since there is likely a medical cause for this child's hallucinations. Behavioral disorders such as oppositional defiant disorder, autism spectrum disorder, or attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder are not characterized by hallucinations and would not present acutely as in this patient. Hyperpyrexia (Choice C) can cause hallucinations in patients, but hyperpyrexia typically refers to hyperthermia greater than 41.0°C (106.0°F). This patient's degree of fever is within the expected range and would be unlikely to cause hallucinations. Increased intracranial pressure (Choice D) can be caused by tumors, intracranial hemo hage, meningitis with impaired cerebrospinal fluid drainage, or other pathologies and can present with neurologic deficits, altered mental status, and seizures. Increased intracranial pressure would be less likely to cause hallucinations. Neuronal inflammation (Choice E) such as in encephalitis can be caused by herpes simplex virus, which may present with fever, headache, seizures, focal neurologic signs, and confusion. Imaging may show inflammation in the temporal lobe. Patients with encephalitis may hallucinate, but this is rare, and this patient's hallucinations are more likely caused by dextromethorphan. .... questions.stem: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after his brother found him agitated, tremulous, and complaining of a headache. He had eaten a peanut butter sandwich 12 hours before his symptoms began. He has a 10-year history of severe major depressive disorder. During this period, he has attempted suicide three times despite treatment with both selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants. Eight days ago, he went to a new physician because of increasing despondency. This physician immediately discontinued the fluoxetine and substituted tranylcypromine to start 1 week later. He also instructed the patient to start a monoamine oxidase inhibitor diet immediately. Cu ent medications also include diphenhydramine as needed for seasonal allergies. He has no history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, or migraine. On a ival, he is agitated. His temperature is 40.6°C (105°F), pulse is 130/min, respirations are 26/min, and blood pressure is 180/120 mm Hg. Physical examination shows diaphoresis and tremor of the upper extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are 3+ bilaterally; there are no pathologic reflexes. On mental status examination, he is complaining of occipital headache. He is oriented to person but not to place or time. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Combining MAOIs with other serotonergic agents or starting MAOIs within 2 weeks of discontinuing another serotonergic drug increases the risk for serotonin syndrome. Serotonin syndrome, the result of serotonin toxicity, can lead to the triad of acute confusion, autonomic hyperactivity (hyperthermia, tachycardia, hypertension), and neuromuscular excitability (tremor, myoclonus, hype eflexia).

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), which are structurally similar to biogenic amines, bind to monoamine oxidase and inhibit its enzymatic degradation of biogenic amines. This increases synaptic concentrations of serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine. The MAOIs are effective for depression and anxiety but are typically used only after other agents have failed because of the risk for serious adverse effects. The MAOIs decrease the peripheral degradation of tyramine (a sympathomimetic biogenic amine that does not cross the blood-brain ba ier), which may lead to hypertensive crisis if combined with dietary tyramine (eg, cheese, wine). Therefore, patients on MAOIs must adhere to a low-tyramine diet. Combining MAOIs with other serotonergic agents or starting MAOIs within 2 weeks of discontinuing another serotonergic drug also increases the risk for serotonin syndrome. Serotonin syndrome, the result of serotonin toxicity, can lead to the triad of acute confusion, autonomic hyperactivity (hyperthermia, tachycardia, hypertension), and neuromuscular excitability (tremor, myoclonus, hype eflexia). As a result of serotonin receptors in the gastrointestinal tract, vomiting and dia hea can also occur. Management of serotonin syndrome includes discontinuation of all serotonergic agents (the MAOI in this case), supportive care of autonomic instability, sedation with benzodiazepines, intravenous fluid repletion, and cooling measures. Cyproheptadine is an antidote with nonspecific serotonin antagonist properties that can be used, but evidence of efficacy is mixed.

body: A, B, D, and E. . - - - - - - - - - - - - - . - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - .... Acute anxiety attacks (Choice A) or panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and is associated with four additional physical symptoms or associated mental states. These additional symptoms may include heart palpitations or tachycardia, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, the sensation of choking, gastrointestinal distress, paresthesias, sweating, chills, trembling, derealization, the fear of dying, and/or the fear of losing control. Acute fluoxetine withdrawal (Choice B) and withdrawal from other selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) can cause antidepressant discontinuation syndrome, characterized by flu-like symptoms, lethargy, insomnia, nausea, imbalance, and sensory disturbances. It is typically benign and self-limiting, in contrast to serotonin syndrome which is life-threatening and severe. Ingestion of peanut butter (Choice D) would be unlikely to cause this patient's pyrexia, hype eflexia, tachycardia, and hypertension. The patient ingested peanut butter 12 hours before his symptoms began, making a relationship unlikely. Diphenhydramine (Choice E) is a first-generation H 1 receptor blocking agent that is commonly used for the treatment of allergies, insomnia, and motion sickness. Main adverse effects of diphenhydramine arise because of its central nervous system H 1 receptor blockade, anticholinergic, and anti-a-adrenergic adverse effects. This results in a constellation of symptoms such as dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, hallucinations, delirium, ataxia, flushed skin, visual disturbances (cycloplegia), and sedation.

questions.stem: A previously healthy 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of crying spells, poor concentration, fatigue, and lethargy. She says that she sleeps at least 12 hours daily and is no longer interested in socializing. She recently broke up with her boyfriend of 10 months, and her work performance has begun to decline; she is afraid that she will lose her job if it does not improve. She does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Menses occur at regular 28- to 30-day intervals. Her last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago. She has had a 9-kg (20-lb) weight gain over the past 9 months. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 66 kg (145 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a sad mood and restricted affect. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Symptoms of MDD include 2 or more weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). Though neurovegetative symptoms typically manifest as decreased appetite and sleep, patients with a subtype of MDD called atypical depression experience hyperphagia and hypersomnolence. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient meets criteria for major depressive disorder (MDD). Symptoms of MDD include 2 or more weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia (eg, decreased interest in socializing), guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms disrupt everyday functioning. Though neurovegetative symptoms typically manifest as decreased appetite and sleep, patients with a subtype of MDD called atypical depression experience hyperphagia and hypersomnolence. Patients with atypical depression may possess an increased symptom burden and an increased risk for suicide. The most effective treatment includes both psychotherapy and antidepressant medication.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Adjustment disorder with depressed mood (Choice A) refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor with associated depressive symptoms that do not meet the diagnostic criteria for MDD. This patient meets the diagnostic criteria of MDD. Dysthymic disorder (Choice B), more commonly known as persistent depressive disorde efers to depressive symptoms lasting longer than 2 years. Patients with dysthymic disorder do not meet MDD criteria. questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his two friends because of unusual behavior that began at a party 1 hour ago. His friends say that he became raucous and made an unwanted sexual advance to a girl he had just met. He then ran into the street, careless to oncoming cars, saying he was able to avoid them. His pulse is 84/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. He is agitated when left alone, but he calms down when spoken to by nursing staff. Physical examination shows an unsteady gait. Pupils are equal and reactive. Which of the following is the most likely drug taken?
questions.educationalObjective: Alcohol intoxication presents with slu ed speech, ataxia, and emotional !ability. Treatment is supportive and should focus on providing a safe environment and ensuring that patients stay hydrated and maintain a patent airway. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Alcohol is the most likely substance that the patient has ingested. Alcohol is metabolized by zero order kinetics in the liver by the enzyme alcohol dehydrogenase. Individuals with infrequent alcohol use are at greate isk for developing classic symptoms of alcohol intoxication including emotional !ability, disinhibition, slu ed speech, ataxia, and even coma and blackouts. Clinical findings often demonstrate an alcohol scent on the breath, normally reactive pupils, slu ing of speech, gait instability, slow respirations, frequent urination, and behavioral disturbances. Loss of inhibition is a feature of many drugs but is particularly common with alcohol consumption. Treatment should focus on maintaining a safe environment, protecting the patient and others from harm , and monitoring and managing the patient's airway and breathing in the event of emesis, oversedation, or coma. Treatment should also include supporting hydration and, in cases of chronic or severe intoxication, repleting any deficient vitamins and electrolytes. In severe cases of alcohol intoxication that lead to coma, intubation may be required to protect the airway.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answer
body: B, C, D, E, and F. Amphetamine (Choice B) ingestion leads to elevated mood, loss of inhibition, hyperpyrexia, sympathetic excess, and increased thirst. It does not often affect coordination, so patients typically do not present with ataxia. In severe intoxication, excess consumption of isotonic fluids can lead to life-threatening hyponatremia. Cocaine (Choice C) ingestion causes elevated mood, pressured speech, tachycardia, and hypertension. Nose bleeds may occur. Patients do not demonstrate ataxia. . . . . . - . -. -. . .. Heroin (Choice D) use can present along a spectrum of severity depending upon the route of ingestion (intravenous or intranasal) and the dose, but often presents with stupor, a low respiratory rate, and constricted pupils. In instances where there is suspicion of narcotic overdose with respiratory depression, airway compromise, or coma, naloxone should be given empirically. There is no antidote, but consumption is rarely dangerous. Supportive care is appropriate in the event of agitation. Marijuana (Choice F) intoxication presents with red eyes, hyperphagia, and mood changes which may range from lassitude to paranoia.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy is brought for a school physical examination. He was diagnosed with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder 2 years ago and was treated with methylphenidate therapy for 3 months with moderate symptom resolution. Methylphenidate was discontinued because of persistent loss of appetite and weight loss. Desipramine therapy was started at 25 mg daily and gradually increased to 100 mg daily. His symptoms have improved, and he has had no adverse effects. An ECG shows a QTc of 0.5 sec (N:s;0.44 ). Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Stimulants are first-line medications for ADHD, and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, a 2-adrenergic agonists, serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, and tricyclic antidepressants are second-line treatments. Tricyclic antidepressants increase the risk for QTc prolongation and should be discontinued if QTc prolongation occurs. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) presents with chronic symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity and/or inattention that occur in more than one setting and impair academic, social, and/or emotional function. Children with predominant hyperactivity symptoms are unable to sit still and may have difficulty taking turns (postulated to result from a dysregulated dopamine reward pathway), whereas children with predominant inattentive symptoms tend to daydream, process information slowly, and demonstrate difficulty in completing tasks (related to norepinephrine dysregulation). Stimulants, which include amphetamine salts and methylphenidate, are first-line agents that increase synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine. In patients unable to tolerate stimulants, second-line agents include norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (eg, atomoxetine), a2-adrenergic agonists (eg, clonidine, guanfacine), serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (eg, venlafaxine), and tricyclic antidepressants (eg, desipramine, imipramine). Tricyclic antidepressants are known to increase the QTc interval. In patients with prolonged QTc intervals, the offending medication should be discontinued (especially in ADHD, for which multiple alternative medication options exist).

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. 24-Hour ambulatory ECG monitoring (Choice A) or echocardiography (Choice B) would be unnecessary if the offending medication is discontinued. QTc prolongation related to tricyclic antidepressant medication is typically reversible. Further cardiac investigation should be delayed until verifying that the patient's QTc normalizes after desipramine discontinuation. Increasing the desipramine dose (Choice D) would be unnecessary and dangerous, since this patient's ADHD symptoms have remitted and his QTc is prolonged. Switching to bupropion therapy (Choice E), an antidepressant, would be inappropriate. Increasing the desipramine dose (Choice D) would be unnecessary and dangerous, since this patient's ADHD symptoms have remitted and his QTc is prolonged. Switching to bupropion therapy (Choice E), an antidepressant, would be inappropriate. Bupropion is generally used for depression as a monotherapy, in adjunct to other antidepressants, and as a smoking cessation aid. Its efficacy in ADHD has not been conclusively demonstrated. Known side effects include lowering the seizure threshold, and cardiac dysrhythmias for which this patient is already at risk given the prolonged QTc.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of a 1-month history of increasingly "paranoid" behavior. He is convinced that his wife is trying to kill him. His wife reports that he has been reluctant to eat food unless the containers are opened in front of him. He has Parkinson disease, which has been well controlled with carbidopa-levodopa for 4 years. Trials of reducing the dose have resulted in an inability to walk because of significant worsening of his gait. He takes no other medications. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 83/min, respirations are 19/min, and blood pressure is 125/86 mm Hg. Neurologic examination shows mild bradykinesia and diffuse rigidity; no tremor is noted. There is mild involuntary ankle rotation bilaterally. On mental status examination, he is calm and oriented to person and place but not to time or date. He has a low mood and blunted affect. He is reluctant to speak with his wife present. When alone, he briefly indicates that his wife is not to be trusted and will not give further details. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Parkinson disease is associated with cognitive impairments, psychosis, sleep disorders, and dementia. Psychosis typically manifests as hallucinations, paranoia, and delusions. Atypical antipsychotics are recommended. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Parkinson disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by degradation of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra. Symptoms are associated with an imbalance of dopamine and acetylcholine, resulting in the characteristic findings of bradykinesia, resting tremor, muscle rigidity, and postural instability. Psychiatric disturbances, sleep disorders, and dementia are common complications in addition to prominent motor symptoms. Personality changes such as isolation, withdrawal, anhedonia, and apathy are typically observed earlier in the course of disease, with emergence of depression, loss of impulse control, paranoia, psychosis (hallucinations, delusions), and dementia as the disease progresses. These symptoms can be very distressing for the patient, family members, and caregivers, and pharmacologic therapy is indicated. Treatment should be tailored to the prevailing symptoms. For this patient, the presence of paranoia and delusions suggest use of the atypical antipsychotic quetiapine would be of benefit. Quetiapine displays activity as a dopamine, serotonin, and histamine antagonist, and the degree of dopamine antagonism has not been shown to significantly worsen Parkinson symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Gabapentin (Choice A) is an antiepileptic medication commonly used in treatment of neuropathic pain. It has been shown to initially improve motor symptoms and tremor in patients with Parkinson disease. This patient has motor symptoms well-controlled with carbidopa-levodopa and instead requires treatment directed toward psychosis. Haloperidol (Choice B) and other first-generation antipsychotics are contraindicated in patients with Parkinson disease since the degree - - - - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- - - -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- - - - - - - .... Quetiapine displays activity as a dopamine, serotonin, and histamine antagonist, and the degree of dopamine antagonism has not been shown to significantly worsen Parkinson symptoms. Inco ect Answers: A, B, C, and E. Gabapentin (Choice A) is an antiepileptic medication commonly used in treatment of neuropathic pain. It has been shown to initially improve motor symptoms and tremor in patients with Parkinson disease. This patient has motor symptoms well-controlled with carbidopa-levodopa and instead requires treatment directed toward psychosis. Haloperidol (Choice B) and other first-generation antipsychotics are contraindicated in patients with Parkinson disease since the degree of dopaminergic blockade can severely worsen symptoms to the point of increased psychosis, worsening parkinsonism or dystonia, encephalopathy, catatonia, and death. Modafinil (Choice C) is a stimulant that may be used to treat sleep disturbances and excessive daytime sleepiness in patients with Parkinson disease. It is not indicated for treatment of psychosis. Valproic acid (Choice E) is an antiepileptic medication indicated for a large range of psychiatric and neurologic disorders. A rare complication is drug-induced Parkinsonism, with development of bradykinesia, resting tremor, postural instability, rigidity, and cognitive impairment. It should be avoided in this patient.

questions.stem: A previously healthy 57-year-old woman comes to the physician with her husband because of an 8-week history of progressive confusion and memory loss. She is no longer able to work at her job as a public relations manager because she has become so withdrawn. Her husband says that she was previously outgoing and talkative but now hardly says a word and is unable to comprehend simple conversations. Her speech is dysarthric. Examination shows diffuse hype eflexia and prominent snout, grasp, and palmomental reflexes. Babinski sign is present bilaterally. She exhibits startle myoclonus to loud noises. Her Mini-Mental State Examination score is 8/30. Laboratory studies, including cerebrospinal fluid analysis, are within normal limits. An MRI of the brain shows no abnormalities. EEG shows generalized slowing with periodic sharp waves. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: CJD is a human prion disease that presents with rapidly progressing dementia, neuropsychiatric symptoms, cerebellar dysfunction, corticospinal tract dysfunction, basal ganglia dysfunction, and stimulus-induced myoclonus. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD) is a human prion disease in which prion proteins replicate throughout the brain and cause neurotoxicity. Patients typically present with neuropsychiatric symptoms (eg, dementia, anxiety, deficits in higher cortical functions such as aphasia and apraxia, frontal release symptoms such as behavioral disturbance with reemergence of the primitive reflexes), cerebellar manifestations (eg, ataxia), corticospinal tract dysfunction (eg, spastic weakness, Babinski sign, hype eflexia), and basal ganglia signs (eg, hypokinesia, rigidity). Stimulus-induced myoclonus is a specific sign of CJD. Over time, the dementia rapidly progresses, invariably leading to death within a year. A probable CJD diagnosis can be made clinically, though an autopsy showing spongiform degeneration of the cortex confirms the diagnosis. An EEG showing repetitive, periodic epileptiform discharges supports the diagnosis. Treatment is supportive (eg, hospice evaluation, education, benzodiazepines for myoclonus).

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: B, C, D, and E. Dementia, Alzheimer type (Choice B) is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment, progresses to apraxia and language abnormalities, and culminates in behavioral and personality changes preventing the patient from performing basic activities of daily living. Rapidly progressive deficits, corticospinal tract dysfunction, frontal disinhibition early in the disease course, and stimulus-induced myoclonus would be atypical. Multiple sclerosis (Choice C) is an autoimmune demyelinating disease that presents with focal neurologic deficits separated in space and time. Rapidly progressive dementia, diffuse and symmetric corticospinal tract dysfunction, and stimulus-induced myoclonus would be atypical. .... Mark . - . . . . . . - E) Pick disease Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD) is a human prion disease in which prion proteins replicate throughout the brain and cause neurotoxicity. Patients typically present with neuropsychiatric symptoms (eg, dementia, anxiety, deficits in higher cortical functions such as aphasia and apraxia, frontal release symptoms such as behavioral disturbance with reemergence of the primitive reflexes), cerebellar manifestations (eg, ataxia), corticospinal tract dysfunction (eg, spastic weakness, Babinski sign, hype eflexia), and basal ganglia signs (eg, hypokinesia, rigidity). Stimulus-induced myoclonus is a specific sign of CJD. Over time, the dementia rapidly progresses, invariably leading to death within a year. A probable CJD diagnosis can be made clinically, though an autopsy showing spongiform degeneration of the cortex confirms the diagnosis. An EEG showing repetitive, periodic epileptiform discharges supports the diagnosis. Treatment is supportive (eg, hospice evaluation, education, benzodiazepines for myoclonus). Inco ect Answers: B, C, D, and E. Dementia, Alzheimer type (Choice B) is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment, progresses to apraxia and language abnormalities, and culminates in behavioral and personality changes preventing the patient from performing basic activities of daily living. Rapidly progressive deficits, corticospinal tract dysfunction, frontal disinhibition early in the disease course, and stimulus-induced myoclonus would be atypical. Multiple sclerosis (Choice C) is an autoimmune demyelinating disease that presents with focal neurologic deficits separated in space and time. Rapidly progressive dementia, diffuse and symmetric corticospinal tract dysfunction, and stimulus-induced myoclonus would be atypical. Parkinson disease (Choice D) presents with dysfunction of voluntary movements in the form of bradykinesia, resting tremo igidity, and postural instability. Dementia may occur late in the disease course. Dementia early in the disease course, frontal release signs, corticospinal tract dysfunction, and stimulus-induced myoclonus would be atypical. Pick disease (Choice E), or frontotemporal dementia, is a neurodegenerative disorder characterized by atrophy of the frontal and temporal lobes, causing deficits in social behavior or personality along with dementia. Rapidly progressive deficits, corticospinal tract dysfunction, and stimulus-induced myoclonus would be atypical.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man with dementia, Alzheimer type, is brought to the physician because of a 4-day history of visual hallucinations and i itability. He has a 2-month history of insomnia. He has osteoarthritis, hypertension, and hypothyroidism. His medications are rivastigmine, ibuprofen, atenolol, levothyroxine, and amitriptyline. His blood pressure is 128/88 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he recalls zero of three objects after 5 minutes. During the examination, he points his finger and sternly says, "You need to stop doing that right this minute." When asked whom he is addressing, he says, "that tiny little man shouting over in the corner." Results of a complete blood count, measurement of serum glucose concentration, and kidney and liver function tests are within the reference ranges. Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. Discontinuation of which of the following of this patient's medications is most appropriate at this time?
questions.educationalObjective: Delirium is an acute confusional state resulting from acute medical illness, sleep disturbances, substance use, and/or medications that most commonly occurs in older patients and patients with dementia. questions.retrievalTag: Admit her to a psychiatric hospital
questions.stem: A 17-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her parents because of a 20-kg (45-lb) weight loss during the past 6 months. She also has become more isolated from her friends and has lost interest in activities she used to enjoy. Her academic performance has deteriorated from excellent to average. She has major depressive disorder treated with fluoxetine for 3 years. She recently stopped taking it because of concern that it was preventing her from losing weight. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 41 kg (90 lb); BMI is 16 kg/m2. Her pulse is 46/min, and palpable systolic blood pressure is 86 mm Hg. On mental status examination, she says she has been feeling poorly lately but has attributed it to stress at school and pressure from her parents to complete college applications. She is pleased that she has lost weight but feels that she has "more to lose." She says her appetite has not decreased and that she feels hungry a good part of the time. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation. She is adamantly opposed to beginning fluoxetine again. She is not interested in counseling and does not think there is "anything wrong" with her. Her parents want her to be admitted to a psychiatric hospital, but she is opposed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Admit her to a psychiatric hospital

questions.educationalObjective: Anorexia nervosa is characterized by an intense fear of gaining weight, decreased self-worth related to body weight, and consequent restrictive eating or binge eating and purging behavior (eg, vomiting, laxative, or diuretic misuse). Physical examination may show bradycardia and hypotension. Patients with unstable vital signs or evidence of organ dysfunction require inpatient hospitalization. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Anorexia nervosa is characterized by an intense fear of gaining weight, decreased self-worth related to body weight, and consequent restrictive eating or binging and purging behavior (eg, vomiting, laxative or diuretic misuse). Patients with anorexia nervosa often have a BMI of less than 17 kg/m 2. Physical examination can show bradycardia, hypotension, dry, scaly skin, and fine hair or hair loss. Insufficient nutrition and weight loss may lead to ameno hea. Anorexia nervosa may lead to serious medical complications including seizure, cardiac a hythmia, heart failure, liver failure, pancreatitis, and severe electrolyte imbalance. Treatment of anorexia requires a combined medical and psychiatric approach with behavioral and pharmacologic therapy. First-line pharmacologic therapy is with atypical antipsychotic agents, such as olanzapine. Patients who manifest unstable vital signs or evidence of organ damage require inpatient hospitalization in a psychiatric or combined medical psychiatric hospital.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man has had tension and hand tremors for 1 month. He has been treated for bipolar disorder with lithium carbonate for 6 months. He has a 2-year history of alcohol abuse on weekends. His temperature is 36. ?°C (98°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 140/86 mm Hg. Examination shows mild tremors of the fingers and hands. Serum lithium carbonate concentration is 1 mEq/L (therapeutic range=0.6-1.2). Serum electrolyte concentrations and thyroid function tests are within normal limits.
questions.educationalObjective: Lithium commonly causes a mild, symmetric tremor of the upper extremities. In patients with functional impairment related to the tremor, propranolol is used. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Lithium is a mood stabilizing medication commonly utilized to treat acute mania and as maintenance treatment in bipolar disorder (to prevent the recu ence of manic or depressive episodes). At therapeutic doses, lithium can cause a variety of adverse effects, including tremor, nystagmus, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, and thyroid dysfunction. Lithium-related tremors typically present with mild, symmetric, action tremors of the upper extremities. The management of lithium-related tremor may involve watchful waiting or, in patients with moderate to severe functional impairment, the addition of a -adrenergic blocker such as propranolol. Acute lithium toxicity presents with neuromuscular excitability (coarse tremor, confusion, hype eflexia), nausea, vomiting, and dysrhythmias.

questions.stem: An otherwise healthy 25-year-old man comes to the physician because he has a severe fear of flying and must attend an essential business meeting in Europe in 2 days. He has no substance abuse problems or other mental or physical problems.
questions.educationalObjective: Specific phobia is a psychiatric disorder featuring anxiety about a certain situation or object that leads to avoidance behavior. Benzodiazepines are appropriate for one-time use in patients with infrequent anxiety related to a specific phobia, but cognitive behavioral therapy is the most effective and safe long-term treatment for specific phobia. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Specific phobia is a psychiatric disorder featuring anxiety about a certain situation or object that leads to avoidance behavior. Common phobias include flying, animals, and enclosed spaces. Management depends on the patient's degree of impairment. If the feared situation arises frequently such that the patient's avoidance causes chronic impairment, cognitive behavioral therapy that includes graded exposure to the feared situation is most effective (benzodiazepine use may impede the extinction of a patient's learned fea esponse by teaching the patient they need the benzodiazepine to endure the situation). However, for patients with phobias of situations that occur infrequently (eg, one-time flights) or patients without access to cognitive behavioral therapy, a small supply of benzodiazepines (eg, lorazepam) can be helpful. Given that benzodiazepines increase the risk for falls, sedation, cognitive impairment, and addiction, benzodiazepines should be used with caution in older patients and patients with substance use disorders.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with Down syndrome is brought to the physician because of increasing forgetfulness and i itability over the past 10 months. He has been awakening at 3 am to get dressed for the day and now gets lost when he takes his daily walk. He appears disheveled. Examination shows decreased memory and a normal mood and affect. During the interview, he attempts to leave the office, saying "take me home."
questions.educationalObjective: Dementia, Alzheimer type, involves short term memory loss combined with impairment of at least two cognitive domains. The nucleus basalis of Meynert contains a high concentration of acetylcholine-rich neurons. Destruction of these neurons has been implicated in the pathophysiology of dementia, Alzheimer type, and pharmacologic treatments include the use of acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, which attempt to maintain acetylcholine signaling despite a relative paucity of the neurotransmitter.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: The nucleus basalis of Meynert is affected in dementia, Alzheimer type, a common variety of dementia that presents with impairment in one or more functional domains that negatively impacts an individual's ability to function in daily life. The nucleus basalis of Meynert lies in the substantia in nominate just anterior to the anterior commissure and is rich in acetylcholine. Degeneration of this area, with subsequent loss of acetylcholine signaling, has been linked to dementia, Alzheimer type. Patients with this dementia invariably present with short-term memory deficits, but a clinical diagnosis necessitates simultaneous impairment in at least two cognitive domains including language, visuospatial capabilities, behavior or personality, and judgment. This change should be confirmed by an objective third party who knows the patient's baseline. The diagnosis can be challenging in patients with underlying Down syndrome, although dementia, Alzheimer type, is common in these patients. First-line pharmacotherapy includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, which slow the degradation of acetylcholine.

Amygdaloid nucleus (Choice A) is part of the olfactory and limbic systems and lies in the anterior part of the temporal lobe. It has connections to the hypothalamus, hippocampus, and cingulate gyrus. It is not known to play a role in dementia, Alzheimer type. Caudate nucleus (Choice B) is part of the basal ganglia and lies next to the thalamus. It receives dopaminergic input from the nigrostriatal tract. Damage to the caudate nucleus is implicated in Huntington disease. Medial geniculate nucleus (Choice C) lies within the thalamus and is a relay point between the inferior colliculus and the auditory cortex. It is not implicated in dementia, Alzheimer type. Red nucleus (Choice E) lies in the midbrain and is part of an accessory motor pathway, although the dominant corticospinal tract is more important in primates. The red nucleus is not affected in dementia, Alzheimer type. Substantia nigra (Choice F) is located in the midbrain and contains a high concentration of dopaminergic neurons. Destruction of these neurons leads to Parkinson disease. Subthalamic nucleus (Choice G) lies medial to the internal capsule and dorsal to the substantia nigra. Unilateral strokes affecting this nucleus can cause hemiballismus.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old man is brought to the physician because of a change in personality over the past 4 months. He has become sexually disinhibited, tactless, impulsive, and easily i itated. His father had similar symptoms at the age of 42 years. Examination shows involuntary movements of the tongue. He has a sudden coarse involuntary jerking movement of the upper extremity that he attempts to hide by pretending to straighten his hair.
questions.educationalObjective: HD is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by a trinucleotide repeat expansion within the Huntingtin gene leading to an abe ant protein product. The disease presents with involuntary movements and often with psychiatric illness. It is associated with atrophy of the caudate nuclei in the basal ganglia. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Caudate nucleus is most likely affected in this patient who probably has Huntington disease (HD). HD is a heritable progressive neurodegenerative disorder typically arising in the third to sixth decades of life associated with atrophy of the caudate nuclei in the basal ganglia. The disease usually presents with an insidious onset of psychiatric (i itability, depression, psychosis) and cognitive symptoms. Motor symptoms (chorea, hypotonia, dystonia, loss of voluntary control of movements) typically appear later in the disease course. People with HD typically die 10 to 40 years after disease onset. Chorea, a key HD symptom, can be initially experienced as restlessness, and patients with involuntary movements often try to conceal them by transitioning into purposeful movements. Treatment is symptomatic and can include tetrabenazine or deutetrabenazine, which target chorea by depleting dopamine stores. Since HD is genetic, a family history is common, and patients can demonstrate anticipation, a genetic inheritance phenomenon in which future generations are affected at a younger age or with more severe symptoms.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman comes to the physician at her husband's request because of increasing i itability and tense mood over the past year. Her husband would like to start a family, and he is concerned about her ability to handle the added responsibility. She says that she wo ies about their finances despite the fact that they have no debt and she and her husband have jobs that pay well. Her pulse is 84/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities except for sweaty palms. Mental status examination shows tension and anxiety. She admits to occasional difficulty sleeping and periods of mild depression but does not think that this is out of the ordinary. Laboratory studies show: Hematocrit Erythrocyte count Leukocyte count Serum Thyroid-stimulating hormone Thyroxine (T 4) 38% 4 million/mm3 5500/mm3 0.5 µU/mL 12 µg/dL Toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Buspirone stimulates the 5HT1 A receptors on the neurons in the serotonergic pathways of the brain to treat generalized anxiety disorder. Unlike SSRIs, it does not cause adverse sexual effects, and unlike benzodiazepines, it does not cause addiction or tolerance. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Generalized anxiety disorde equires the presence of excessive wo y about numerous aspects of daily life occu ing on more days than not for at least 6 months, with consequent inability to control symptoms of anxiety, and with the presence of at least three other symptoms (i itability, muscle tension, sleep problems, poor concentration, fatigue, restlessness). The symptoms must not be attributable to another organic cause. It may be associated with sympathetic hyperactivity, such as sweaty palms, or panic attacks. Buspirone is one potential treatment of generalized anxiety disorder. Buspirone acts on the serotonergic neurons in the brain to stimulate serotonin receptors. It is particularly useful since it does not cause addiction, sedation, or tolerance. In this case, it is favored over a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) since these medications are sometimes accompanied by sexual dysfunction which may be unfavorable in a patient attempting to conceive.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 4-hour history of violent, agitated behavior. Her college roommates are concerned that she may have used illicit drugs. She has no known history of similar behavior or serious illness. She takes no medications. Her temperature is 38°C (100.4 °F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. On mental status examination, her speech is loud and rapid in rate and rhythm. She describes her mood as "wild"; her affect is reactive but primarily exuberant and i itable. Her thought process is tangential. She reports no hallucinations. During the examination, she tries to leave the emergency department, pushing a security guard aside and throwing a clipboard on the floor. Intoxication with which of the following substances is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Amphetamines are CNS stimulants that cause euphoria and increase sympathetic tone, leading to tachycardia, hypertension, pupillary dilation, and restlessness. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Methamphetamine and other amphetamines are central nervous system (CNS) stimulants and sympathomimetics; intoxication is characterized by euphoria, grandiosity, increased wakefulness and attention, agitation, anorexia, and paranoia. On examination, patients can present with signs of sympathomimetic toxicity including hypertension, tachycardia, tachypnea, diaphoresis, and dilated pupils. As a result of increased synaptic dopamine, hallucinations and paranoia can occur. Though appetite suppression and weight loss are common, electrolyte disturbances would be atypical. Chronic methamphetamine abuse can lead to methamphetamine-induced psychotic disorder, which features chronic delusions, paranoia, and hallucinations. Chronic methamphetamine use may also lead to cognitive impairment. Treatment for acute methamphetamine intoxication is supportive, and benzodiazepines have been shown to be effective for management of agitation or seizures in these patients.

body: A, B, C, and D. Cannabis (Choice A) is a hallucinogen that causes euphoria, paranoia, hallucinations, increased appetite, conjunctiva! injection, and cognitive slowing. questions.retrievalTag: Reassure the father that this is normal development
questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her father for a well-child examination. He is concerned about her weight and eating habits. He says that she is always "on the go" and never sits down for a full meal. She will often just grab a piece of fruit when she is on her way to an activity. She will eat a full meal when the family has dinner together every Sunday. She has talked about wanting to become a vegetarian because of her concern for animals. She has had the same group of friends since elementary school. She is the captain of her soccer team and practices 4 days weekly. She is active in an after-school drama program 3 days weekly and is the lead in this season's play. She maintains a B grade average. Her father says that she talks on the telephone "constantly" and is animated and cheerful most of the time. When she is in her bedroom, she prefers to keep the door closed and stops talking if she is on the telephone and her father walks into the room. She has a disorganized bedroom and often falls fast asleep on a bed covered with piles of clothes. Menarche has not occu ed. She appears thin and muscular. She is at the 50th percentile for height and 35th percentile for weight, which is unchanged from last year. Vital signs are within normal limits. Breast and pubic hair development are Tanner stage 2. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. When interviewed alone, she is animated. She shrugs and rolls her eyes when asked about her diet and weight. She thinks she is fine and does not understand why her father is so upset. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Reassure the father that this is normal development

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Recommend that the father keep a log of what his daughter eats

questions.educationalObjective: Changes to eating patterns and social interactions occur commonly in adolescence. While most changes are benign and only require reassurance, there are some disorders that require intervention. Anorexia nervosa is characterized by an intense fear of gaining weight, decreased self-worth related to body weight, and consequent binge eating and purging behavior or overall oral intake restriction. Bulimia nervosa involves cycles of uncontrollable eating and compensatory behaviors, and both disorders require a combined medical and psychiatric approach to treatment. ... ...

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Reassuring the father that this is normal development is the most appropriate next step, as this patient shows no significant signs of an eating disorder or psychological issue. Young female patients are at increased risk for developing anorexia nervosa and bulimia nervosa. Anorexia nervosa is classically characterized by an intense fear of gaining weight, decreased self-worth related to body weight, and consequent binge eating and purging behavior (eg, vomiting, laxative or diuretic misuse) or overall oral intake restriction. Patients with anorexia nervosa often have a BMI of less than 17 kg/m 2. As a result of insufficient nutrition and weight loss, the hypothalamus decreases gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion, which leads to ameno hea and pubertal delay/a est. The physical examination can show dry, scaly skin and fine hair or hair loss. Bulimia nervosa involves cycles of uncontrollable eating and compensatory behaviors that occur at least once a week over 3 months or more. These patients typically have a normal BMI. Patients that use vomiting as their purging behavior, in either anorexia nervosa or bulimia nervosa, may additionally have signs of dental erosion, knuckle abrasions, hypovolemia, hypokalemia, and hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis. Treatment of disorders is through a combined medical and psychiatric approach and involves co e,cting fluid and electrolyte derangements alongside behavioral and pharmacologic therapy. Additionally, both disorders can be accompanied by major depression or generalized anxiety disorder. This patient continues to eat full meals, has a normal and consistent weight percentile, does not exercise excessively, and does not show signs of depression or anxiety. body: B, C, D, and E. Recommending individual psychotherapy (Choice B) would be appropriate in the setting of anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, major depression, or generalized anxiety disorder. Recommending nutritional counseling (Choice C) may be useful if this patient decides to pursue a vegetarian diet, so that she and her family can ensure that she is obtaining all the co ect nutrients. questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department 1 hour after injuring he ight arm. This is her third visit to the emergency department in 6 months. The parents say that she fell. The child is too upset to say how she injured herself. Her parents note that she is overly aggressive in day care, where she is not responding to instructions, and has hit other children. During her previous visit for a wrist injury, an x-ray of the wrist showed normal findings. On examination, the child appears fearful of the examiner, provides limited responses, and is restless and easily distracted. An x-ray of the right humerus shows a closed fracture. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Child abuse should be suspected when children present repeatedly for several injuries, especially when the presentation is inconsistent with the reported mechanism of injury. Long bone fractures can suggest intentional injury, and when child abuse is suspected, immediate involvement of child protective services is necessary.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Risk factors for child abuse include unstable family situations, prior history of abuse, major birth defects, and parental depression. Child abuse should be suspected when children present repeatedly for several injuries, and especially when the location, severity, pattern, or history are inconsistent with the reported mechanism. While toddlers typically demonstrate occasional defiance, and cooperative play is not expected to develop until 4 years of age, this child's aggressive behavior toward other children at daycare is abnormal and raises suspicion for abuse. Fractures that suggest intentional injury include rib fractures, metaphyseal corner fractures, long bone fractures, and certain skull fractures, or any fracture or bruising sustained in an area or location that could not be reasonably expected on the basis of the child's normal ambulatory and play habits. For example, a femur fracture would never be typical in a nonambulatory 6- month-old patient. If child abuse is suspected, child protective services should be contacted immediately.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 1-year history of increasingly frequent episodes of eye blinking and facial grimacing during the past 6 months that now occur several times daily. He also frequently makes grunting and throat-clearing noises. His teacher often sends him out of the room for being disruptive. He says that he will repeatedly shrug one of his shoulders and attempt to hide this behavior by smoothing his hair. He is emba assed by these behaviors and can suppress them with effort, but they often return when he is distracted and are exacerbated by stress. The behaviors do not occur during sleep. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. During the examination, he has several episodes of rapid, forceful eye blinking and throat clearing. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a mildly anxious mood and affect. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Tourette disorder is a neurologic condition characterized by the presence of both motor and phonic tics that manifest in childhood or adolescence. First-line treatment includes behavioral therapy and antipsychotic medications including aripiprazole, fluphenazine, haloperidol, o isperidone. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Tourette disorder is a neurologic condition characterized by the presence of both motor and phonic tics that manifest in childhood or adolescence. Motor tics often include blinking, grimacing, shrugging, or neck movements. Phonic tics often include throat clearing, grunting, sniffing, or other complex vocalizations such as echolalia or coprolalia. Patients often experience a premonitory sensation or urge prior to the expression of the tic that is relieved by the tic's expression. Patients are typically able to suppress the expression of the tic for a period of time, although with significant effort. Treatment often requires a combined approach with pharmacologic therapy and behavioral training. First-line treatment includes antipsychotic medications such as aripiprazole, fluphenazine, haloperidol, o isperidone, though many medications can be appropriate.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, C, D, and E. While ADHD often co-occurs, treatment with stimulants is not first-line since studies have found that these agents worsen tics. Hydroxyzine (Choice B) is a histamine inverse agonist that is useful for the treatment of pruritus, urticaria, and motion sickness. questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman, whose husband died 18 months ago, is admitted to the hospital because of severe headaches for 4 weeks. She is quiet and seems sad. She believes that she has brain cancer, deserves to be punished for her sins, and is being punished by God for past misdeeds. She has had fatigue and a 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss over the past 4 months. Physical and neurologic examinations show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Adjustment disorder with mixed disturbance of emotions and conduct

questions.educationalObjective: Major depressive disorder is common in older adults and may present with somatic symptoms such as headaches or gastrointestinal distress accompanied by classic features of depression including guilt, anhedonia, sleep disturbances, or appetite changes lasting for more than 2 weeks. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Major depressive disorder is the most likely diagnosis. Symptoms of major depressive disorder (MDD) typically include 2 or more weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms disrupt everyday functioning. The presence of somatic symptoms such as headache or gastrointestinal distress are also commonly seen in conjunction with depression. Additionally, this patient's delusion that she is being punished by God is consistent with a potentially accompanying psychotic feature without meeting criteria for schizoaffective disorder. While feelings of guilt and depressed mood are common within the first few months after the death of a loved one, these symptoms typically resolve and do not interfere with daily functioning. She should be assessed for the presence of suicidality and should be started on pharmacotherapy for her depression.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. Adjustment disorder with mixed disturbances of emotions and conduct (Choice A) describes a reaction to a known stressor that occurs within 3 months and presents with a combination of features including depressed mood, anxiety, and abnormal behavior not meeting criteria for a major depressive episode or bipolar disorder. This patient's husband died 18 months ago. Delusional disorder (Choice B) presents with fixed delusions in the absence of other psychiatric symptoms such as hallucinations, disorganization, depression, or mania. Schizophrenia (Choice D) presents with delusions, hallucinations, profound negative symptoms, or disorganized speech or behavior lasting for 6 months, not secondary to substance use, and contributing to profound dysfunction in everyday life. This patient does not meet criteria. questions.stem: A 23-year-old man is brought to the physician by his mother because of increasing conflict between the two of them over the past 6 months. She says that he has become more difficult because of his growing concern about neighbors who he believes abuse the welfare system. He says that he has had special abilities to predict the future since the age of 15 years, but no one in his family believes him. His mother says that he has never had many friends and describes him as a loner. He has not used any drugs. He has not felt sad or depressed and has had no recent change in appetite or sleep disturbances. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. His language is vague and stilted. Which of the following is the most likely personality disorder in this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Schizotypal personality disorder manifests as an enduring pattern of strange or eccentric beliefs and behavior, social detachment or suspiciousness, and abnormal perceptual experiences such as illusions. Social indifference distinguishes schizotypal disorder from cluster B and C disorders, and eccentric beliefs and behavior differentiate the disorder from the other cluster A disorders.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Schizotypal personality disorder is one of the cluster A personality disorders, the odd or eccentric cluster. The disorder is characterized by odd behavior and thinking and a constricted affect. Patients with schizotypal personality disorder may have strange, overly metaphorical or magical thinking, though they fluctuate in the degree of their conviction about these beliefs (which differentiates these beliefs from the fixed delusions of schizophrenia). The strange beliefs may also manifest as eccentric appearance. Patients may experience somatosensory illusions or other abnormal perceptions. Patients with schizotypal personality disorder may appear to lack interest in cultivating relationships and, because of an inability to interpret others' motivations, may be deeply distrustful and anxious around others. Personality disorders are persistently maladaptive ways of relating to the self and to society that typically appear by early adulthood.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because she has had difficulty sleeping at night since her youngest daughter left for college 2 months ago. During the past week, she has been struggling to stay awake at work. She drinks two to three cocktails to help her fall asleep and 3 to 4 oz of vodka in the morning to help calm her nerves. She is often anxious and has frequent heartburn. She was told by her physician that the heartburn is likely caused by her alcohol consumption. She does not want to alter he outine because she has become increasingly anxious and i itable when she has tried to do so. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is mildly anxious but polite and friendly. She says that she is not depressed but that it was difficult for her to adjust when her daughter first left for college. Laboratory studies show: Erythrocyte count 3500/mm 3 Mean corpuscular volume 102 µm 3 Leukocyte count 4500/mm 3 Serum Mg2+ Thyroid-stimulating hormone AST ALT 1.5 mEq/L 3.5 µU/mL 40 U/L 25 U/L An ECG shows a normal sinus rhythm. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Individuals who suffer alcohol use disorder commonly report insomnia as a complication. Alcohol intake decreases the effectiveness of restorative sleep. Alcohol dependence results in sympathetic hyperactivity when the amount of alcohol intake is decreased and can result in a withdrawal syndrome. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: The patient's presentation is concerning for alcohol use disorder with features suggestive of alcohol dependence. Alcohol use disorder is a clinical diagnosis made by the presence of personal, professional, social, emotional, behavioral, and physical disturbances related to the use of alcohol or attempts to decrease intake. Symptoms may be classified as mild, moderate, or severe depending on the number of domains affected. Alcohol dependence refers to the adaptation of the body to a chronically increased blood alcohol concentration that leads to a withdrawal syndrome when alcohol intake is ceased or significantly decreased. Withdrawal symptoms range from mild tremor, i itability, and insomnia to life-threatening autonomic instability, psychosis, delirium, seizures, and death. Patients should be screened for alcohol use disorder with a validated screening tool. Malnutrition and electrolyte abnormalities are .... body: A, C, D, E, and F. Adjustment disorder (Choice A) is characterized by disproportionate anxiety or mood changes associated with an identifiable stressor. This patient's clinical picture and laboratory studies are consistent with alcohol use disorder, which was likely exacerbated by the extrinsic stressor of her daughter leaving for college. Circadian rhythm sleep disorder (Choice C) is a common cause of insomnia characterized by a shifted or i egular sleep-wake cycle relative to a typical day-night cycle. Symptoms include difficulty falling asleep and/or staying awake at appropriate times, fatigue, and mood changes. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice D) is characterized by persistent anxiety that is unrelated to an identifiable stressor. This patient's anxiety and i itability are more likely a result of alcohol use disorder. Major depressive disorder (Choice E) may be exacerbated by extrinsic psychosocial stressors. Diagnosis requires the presence of depressed mood or anhedonia and at least four other associated symptoms (insomnia or hypersomnia, guilt, decreased energy, concentration deficits, weight loss or weight gain, psychomoto etardation, suicidal ideation). Primary insomnia (Choice F) refers to difficulty initiating or maintaining restorative sleep that is not attributable to any identifiable underlying medical or psychiatric condition. Primary insomnia may be exacerbated by extrinsic stressors. This patient's insomnia can be attributed to alcohol use disorder.

questions.stem: A homeless 27-year-old man who is being treated with antipsychotic and antiparkinsonian drugs comes to the emergency department because he feels anxious and has difficulty urinating. He reports seeing visions. He is hyperactive and disoriented to time and place and has difficulty focusing on nearby objects. Physical examination shows dry mouth, flushed skin, and tachycardia at rest. There is no evidence of muscula igidity. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Anticholinergic agents (eg, diphenhydramine, benztropine) commonly cause cognitive impairment, hallucinations, sedation, ataxia, visual disturbances (cycloplegia, or loss of accommodation), dry mouth, tachycardia, urinary retention, constipation, and flushed skin. Offending medications should be discontinued. ....

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Acetylcholine activity in the brain mediates attention and motivation. Anticholinergic agents commonly cause cognitive impairment or delirium. This patient's antiparkinsonian medications are likely anticholinergic agents (eg, benztropine, diphenhydramine) utilized to prevent the extrapyramidal adverse effects of antipsychotic medications. Along with cognitive impairment (leading to this patient's disorientation), anticholinergic effects can include hallucinations, sedation, ataxia, visual disturbances (cycloplegia, or loss of accommodation), dry mouth, tachycardia, urinary retention, constipation, and flushed skin. In patients demonstrating impairing anticholinergic toxicity, anticholinergic medications should be discontinued.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: B, C, D, and E. An extrapyramidal syndrome (Choice B) may result from antipsychotic medication use. Patients typically present with acute dystonic reaction, parkinsonism, and/or akathisia without significant disorientation. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (Choice C) is a life-threatening condition associated with the antagonism of dopamine that presents with altered mental status, muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, and autonomic dysfunction. Dry mouth, flushed skin, difficulty urinating, and vision abnormalities would be atypical. Noncompliance with antipsychotic drug therapy (Choice D) and psychosis due to substance abuse (Choice E) could result in increased hallucinations and/or delusions. Disorientation, dry mouth, flushed skin, difficulty urinating, and vision abnormalities would be atypical.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman comes to the physician with her husband because of an 8-year history of increasing daytime fatigue. She says that she has difficulty remaining awake whenever she sits down to relax. She has never been able to drive more than 30 minutes without becoming so sleepy that she needs to pull the car over and stop. Her husband says that once every 2 months, she gets up in the middle of the night and does not respond when he calls her name. He says that she does not snore. The patient reports that in high school she trained herself not to laugh because she felt weak and could not remain standing after her friends told jokes. She has no history of serious illness. Her only medication is an oral contraceptive. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she appears concerned and has a reactive affect. She is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Keeping a sleep log for 2 weeks

questions.educationalObjective: Narcolepsy should be suspected in patients with daytime somnolence, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, or hypnagogic hallucinations. Diagnosis is made by polysomnography. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Polysomnography is likely to confirm the diagnosis of narcolepsy in this patient. Narcolepsy commonly presents with hypnagogic hallucinations, daytime somnolence, sleep hallucinations, and cataplexy (muscle weakness triggered by strong emotions). The pathogenesis of narcolepsy involves the loss of orexin-A and orexin-B, which are neurotransmitters found primarily in the hypothalamus that promote wakefulness and inhibit rapid eye movement (REM) sleep. Loss of these neurotransmitters permits abnormal transition from wakefulness to REM sleep with the consequent development of hallucinations and other features of narcolepsy, including cataplexy and sleep paralysis. Diagnosis can be made in patients with daytime somnolence and cataplexy or, in cases where cataplexy is not present, with polysomnography. Polysomnography is a sleep study performed using specialized equipment to measure oxygenation, end-tidal carbon dioxide, and time spent in various sleep stages. Patients with narcolepsy demonstrate decreased sleep latency and rapid entry into REM sleep. Other findings include spontaneous awakening and long stretches of light, non-REM sleep. Pharmacotherapy is directed as promoting wakefulness and avoiding cataplexy.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, C, and D. Keeping a sleep log for 2 weeks (Choice A) can be helpful for patients who are suspected to have poor sleep hygiene and is used to identify behaviors that may be contributing to poor sleep. It is typically used for patients with insomnia, not excessive sleepiness. MRI of the brain (Choice B) is an unnecessary diagnostic test that is unlikely to provide any meaningful information about this patient's condition. This patient has no focal neurologic deficits and no other symptoms of neurologic disease. - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -- -- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - .... questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with schizoaffective disorder is brought to the emergency department because of a ?-day history of progressive lethargy, loss of appetite, nausea, and leg swelling; she has had jaundice since this morning. Five weeks ago, she was hospitalized for treatment of her psychiatric symptoms. Treatment with three medications was started during hospitalization, but she does not remember their names. She has no history of liver disease or other serious illness. She appears drowsy. Her pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 105/70 mm Hg. There is scleral icterus. Abdominal examination shows tenderness in the right upper quadrant. Neurologic examination shows a fine bilateral hand tremor. Mental status examination shows no evidence of hallucinations or delusions. She is oriented to person, place, and year but not to the day of the week or month. Serum studies show: Bilirubin, total 5 mg/dL Alkaline phosphatase 160 U/L AST 240 U/L ALT 210 U/L Which of the following medications is most likely responsible for these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Valproic acid can be used to treat patients with schizoaffective disorder. Overdose may result in a number of different clinical syndromes including self-limited hepatotoxicity with hyperammonemia, o arely with acute liver failure. Treatment is usually supportive as most symptoms are self-limited. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Valproic acid is the medication that is most likely responsible for this patient's findings. Valproic acid is an anti-epileptic drug and mood stabilizer that is occasionally used to treat patients with schizoaffective or bipolar disorder. Inadvertent or intentional overdose can lead to a number of clinical syndromes, such as hyperammonemia leading to encephalopathy, hepatotoxicity with increased AST and ALT, and cerebral edema with coma. This patient's mental status and tremor may be the result of hyperammonemia, which occurs as a result of accumulation of propionic acid, a metabolite of valproic acid that prevents processing of ammonia through the urea cycle. It is often associated with mildly increased liver enzymes. While rare, acute fulminant hepatotoxicity can occur as a result of valproic acid overdose. Because of this, the patient should undergo additional evaluation of the prothrombin time or INR to assess for coagulopathy. Frequently, hepatotoxicity is self-limited and is treated supportively and with removal of the offending drug.

Clozapine (Choice A) is used to treat refractory schizophrenia. Its major toxicities include agranulocytosis, which can be severe and life threatening in the setting of infection. Rarely, patients can develop myocarditis with the rapid development of heart failure, but this patient's symptoms are not consistent with either presentation. Haloperidol (Choice B) overdose can present with features of anticholinergic toxicity and extrapyramidal symptoms such as akathisia and dystonia, or neuroleptic malignant syndrome. This patient's clinical presentation is not consistent with haloperidol overdose. Lithium carbonate (Choice C) overdose can lead to severe nausea and vomiting with neurologic changes including ataxia, myoclonus, tremor, and agitation or delirium. Prolongation of the QT interval and cardiac a hythmias are also seen. Treatment with hemodialysis may be required. Chronic toxicity may result in hypothyroidism or nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. Trazodone (Choice D) is used to treat depression, not schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder. Clinical signs of overdose include dry mouth, lethargy, nausea or vomiting, and dizziness.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old man comes to the physician because of the inability to maintain an erection through completion of sexual activity. He has been ma ied for 3 months and has not been able to complete coitus over the past 2 months. He is usually able to attain an erection by direct penile stimulation and is often aware of nocturnal erections when waking during the night. For the past month, he and his wife have attempted to have intercourse about twice a week. He drinks several alcoholic beverages before engaging in sexual activity in an effort to relax. He has had two major depressive episodes over the past 4 years and was successfully treated with desipramine; he has not taken the medication since his ma iage. He has borderline hypertension but takes no medications for it. He has a strong family history of type 2 diabetes mellitus. He takes ranitidine for stomach problems. His vital signs are normal. Cranial nerve function appears normal. He walks normally. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 11 g/dl Platelet count 178,000/mm 3 Serum Fasting glucose y-Glutamyltransferase 86 mg/dl 70 U/L (N=5-50) Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Chronic, heavy alcohol use can lead to decreased libido and impaired vasodilation of the vasculature of the corpora cavernosa. Patients with erectile dysfunction related to alcohol use typically demonstrate continued nocturnal erections. ....

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: y-Glutamyltransferase is a liver enzyme that is released secondary to liver damage and is commonly utilized to screen for alcohol use disorder. For y-glutamyltransferase to be increased, patients typically must drink heavily each day for weeks at a time. Alcohol decreases gonadotropin release from the hypothalamus and testosterone release from the testes, resulting in decreased libido. Chronic alcohol use also decreases the production of nitric oxide, decreasing the relaxation of the vasculature of the corpora cavernosa and consequently decreasing erectile function. These mechanisms of erectile dysfunction typically present with continued nocturnal erections, as the nitric oxide concentrations are commonly sufficient to allow for nocturnal erections and erections from direct penile stimulation but insufficient to permit the level of rigidity required for vaginal penetration. Alcohol-induced erectile dysfunction is reversible with abstinence from alcohol. ....
