Alcohol-induced erectile dysfunction is reversible with abstinence from alcohol.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: B, C, D, E, F, and G. Desipramine therapy (Choice B) would not influence sexual performance several months after its discontinuation. Sexual dysfunction related to serotonergic medications is typically reversible with discontinuation of the medication. Diabetes mellitus (Choice C) can lead to erectile dysfunction primarily secondary to microvascular damage. However, patients do not typically demonstrate nocturnal erections, and this patient had a normal fasting glucose. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice D), major depressive disorder (Choice E), and marital conflict (Choice F) can lead to sexual dysfunction with continued nocturnal erections. This patient does not demonstrate evidence of these disorders or marital conflict, instead demonstrating evidence of chronic, heavy alcohol use. Ranitidine (Choice G) is a histamine-2 (H 2) receptor antagonist that decreases gastric acid secretion and is utilized to relieve reflux symptoms or dyspepsia. H2 antagonists may act on the central nervous system to cause somnolence and confusion but do not typically lead to erectile dysfunction.

questions.stem: A ?-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his adoptive parents because of ongoing concern about his behavior and academic performance. He is two grade levels behind his peers in reading and frequently has fights with other children. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His biological mother abused multiple substances before and during pregnancy. On adoption at the age of 15 months, there was some evidence that she may have physically abused him. He is at the 10th percentile for height and 5th percentile for weight. His pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 116/78 mm Hg. Examination shows a flattened nasal bridge, a long philtrum, and prominent ears. During the interview, he is friendly but becomes frustrated and distracted and is unable to sit still. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Alcohol is a known teratogen that causes fetal alcohol syndrome when used during pregnancy. Fetal alcohol syndrome presents with intellectual disability and developmental delays, along with physical malformations such as microcephaly, facial dysmorphia, limb deformities, and congenital heart disease. ... .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Alcohol is a proven teratogen, as alcohol use during pregnancy leads to i eversible fetal neurologic effects and physical malformations in a cluster of symptoms called fetal alcohol syndrome. Fetal alcohol syndrome is a leading cause of intellectual disability in the United States and would explain this patient's poor academic performance. As a result of the failure of cell migration, fetal alcohol syndrome also presents with physical abnormalities such as microcephaly, facial abnormalities (small palpebral fissures, thin vermilion border, cleft lip/palate, smooth or long philtrum), limb deformities, and congenital heart disease. Patients with fetal alcohol syndrome may demonstrate abnormal bone development (eg, cervical spine fusion) and delayed bone growth. Though substance use during pregnancy can result in obstetric complications such as preterm birth, the physical malformations, as those seen in this case, are specific to alcohol use.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, C, D, and E. Angelman syndrome (Choice A) occurs in patients who have paternal disomy (who have inherited both copies of a chromosome from the biological fathe ather than one copy from each parent) of chromosome 15. Angelman syndrome is characterized by short stature, intellectual disability, severe speech impairment, and ataxia. The facial features present in this case are more likely secondary to fetal alcohol syndrome. Fragile X syndrome (Choice C) is an X-linked dominant cause of inherited intellectual disability that presents as a result of a CGG- trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene. It presents with clinical features including intellectual disability, an elongated face with a prominent jaw, a high-arched palate, large ears, hyperextensible joints, and postpubertal macroorchidism. This patient's birth history and constellation of physical features suggest fetal alcohol syndrome instead. .... Inco ect Answers: A, C, D, and E. Angelman syndrome (Choice A) occurs in patients who have paternal disomy (who have inherited both copies of a chromosome from the biological fathe ather than one copy from each parent) of chromosome 15. Angelman syndrome is characterized by short stature, intellectual disability, severe speech impairment, and ataxia. The facial features present in this case are more likely secondary to fetal alcohol syndrome. Fragile X syndrome (Choice C) is an X-linked dominant cause of inherited intellectual disability that presents as a result of a CGG- trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene. It presents with clinical features including intellectual disability, an elongated face with a prominent jaw, a high-arched palate, large ears, hyperextensible joints, and postpubertal macroorchidism. This patient's birth history and constellation of physical features suggest fetal alcohol syndrome instead. Rett disorder (Choice D) is characterized by regression of developmental milestones and occurs exclusively in female patients. After initially normal development, patients lose speech capabilities and motor skills and develop stereotypical hand movements, gait abnormalities, and developmental delay. Autistic features may be present but abnormal facial features are not seen. Shaken baby syndrome (Choice E) is a consequence of nonaccidental trauma inflicted on an infant. Signs may be mild and subtle, such as poor feeding, i itability, or lethargy, or severe and conspicuous, such as coma or seizures. Abnormal facies are not a feature of shaken baby syndrome; retinal hemo hages, rib fractures, and subdural hematomas are more often seen.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of increasingly severe pain that originates in her left shoulder and radiates to her elbow. She describes the pain as constant and burning, rating her cu ent pain as a 7 on a 10-point scale. Eighteen months ago, she sustained a nerve injury of the left upper extremity in a motor vehicle collision. Since that time, she has been unable to return to work. Cu ent medications include oxycodone and gabapentin. Physical examination shows atrophy of the left thenar eminence. Muscle strength in the left forearm and finger flexors is 3/5. On sensory examination, there is severe pain with light stroking of the anterolateral aspect of the left arm. Further sensory testing is defe ed. During the examination she tells her physician, "I'm tired of all this. My medication is not strong enough. It only takes the edge off my pain, which is only getting worse. I'm realizing I'll be like this forever." Which of the following is the most appropriate response by the physician?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: "Are you wo ied about more nerve damage developing?"

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: "Do you ever use more pain medication than is prescribed?"

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: "Have you been feeling like just giving up?"

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: "Is the pain caused by touch socially limiting?"

questions.educationalObjective: Patients with chronic illness have highe ates of attempted and successful suicide. When a patient expresses feelings of despai esignation, or hopelessness, physicians should ask detailed questions about suicidal ideation and recent o emote suicide attempts. .,.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: In this young patient with severe chronic pain who is expressing despair, the physician should conduct a thorough suicide risk assessment including questions about suicidal ideation or attempts. While she does not meet the criteria for major depressive disorder, which include 2 or more weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or feelings of worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance), her expression of being "tired of all this" and "realizing I'll be like this forever" may be indications of depression. Suicide is the most serious and acute risk of patients with depression, so physicians should ask detailed questions about patients' suicidal ideation and recent o emote suicide attempts. The physician should prioritize this line of questioning with the patient as it pertains to her immediate safety and will build rapport with the patient by helping her feel heard and understood.

explanationSections.heading: Inco ect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. "Are you wo ied about more nerve damage developing?" (Choice A) does not address the patient's primary concerns of worsening pain, inadequate relief, and concern for the future. The physician-patient relationship will benefit more if the patient feels understood by the physician. The physician must consider the immediate and future safety of the patient by assessing the risk for suicide. "Do you ever use more pain medication than is prescribed?" (Choice B) is important to ascertain from the patient. However, taking this approach to begin the conversation with the patient may imply distrust and prohibit the patient from engaging further with the physician. Rather than changing the topic from the patient's pain, the physician should investigate whether the patient has any suicidal ideations.
